vim visual !: filters the highlighted lines through an external command via shell_pipe
vim visual D: delete the highlighted lines (linewise)
vim visual X: delete the highlighted lines (linewise)
vim visual Y: yank the highlighted lines (linewise)
vim visual C: delete highlighted lines and start insert (linewise change)
vim visual S: delete highlighted lines and start insert (linewise change)
vim visual R: delete highlighted lines and start insert (linewise change)
vim gx: open file/URL under cursor. Bound to goto_file, which opens URLs externally and files in-editor. Partial: gx in vim is URL-only (gf is files); zmax gx==gf handles both.
vim CTRL-W H: move current window to the far left (swap_view_left)
vim CTRL-W J: move current window to the very bottom (swap_view_down)
vim CTRL-W K: move current window to the very top (swap_view_up)
vim CTRL-W L: move current window to the far right (swap_view_right)
vim CTRL-W R: rotate windows upwards (rotate_view_reverse)
vim CTRL-W S: same as CTRL-W s (hsplit)
vim CTRL-W c: close current window; zmax uses C-w d (wclose)
vim CTRL-W h: go to window left (jump_view_left)
vim CTRL-W j: go to window down (jump_view_down)
vim CTRL-W k: go to window up (jump_view_up)
vim CTRL-W l: go to window right (jump_view_right)
vim CTRL-W o: close all but current window (wonly)
vim CTRL-W q: quit/close current window (wclose)
vim CTRL-W r: rotate windows downwards (rotate_view)
vim CTRL-W s: split window horizontally (hsplit)
vim CTRL-W v: split window vertically (vsplit)
vim CTRL-W w: cycle to next window (rotate_view)
vim CTRL-W x: exchange current window with the next (transpose_view)
vim CTRL-W <Down>: go to window down (jump_view_down)
vim CTRL-W <Up>: go to window up (jump_view_up)
vim CTRL-W <Left>: go to window left (jump_view_left)
vim CTRL-W <Right>: go to window right (jump_view_right)
vim [c: go back to start of previous change/diff hunk (goto_prev_change)
vim [f: same as gf: open the file under the cursor (goto_file)
vim ]c: go forward to start of next change/diff hunk (goto_next_change)
vim ]f: same as gf: open the file under the cursor (goto_file)
vim ga: print the ascii/unicode value of the character under the cursor (bound to :character-info)

auto-info popups
backward-sexp (C-M-b): move over the previous balanced s-expression
forward-sexp (C-M-f): move over the next balanced s-expression

prog-indent-sexp (prog-mode C-M-q, here = s): reindent point .. end-of-forward-sexp via the tree-sitter reindent (indent-region equivalent); with a prefix argument reindent the enclosing defun. Faithful to prog-mode.el (forward-sexp span, prefix -> beginning-of-defun).
backward-up-list (C-M-u): move backward out of the enclosing list to its open (zmax_core::list_motion)
up-list: move forward out of the enclosing list to just past its close (zmax_core::list_motion)
emacs backward-sentence -> move_sentence_backward (goto_para_impl over movement::move_prev_sentence)
emacs forward-sentence (M-e) -> move_sentence_forward (movement::move_next_sentence)
what-line: report the line number of point
emacs indent-relative -> indent_relative: space out to under the next indent point in the previous nonblank line, else advance to the next tab stop
emacs what-page -> what_page: report the page number (^L-delimited) and the line within that page (zmax_core::page::page_and_line)
count-lines-page (C-x l): lines on the current page, before + after point
describe-char (C-u C-x =): char glyph + dec/oct/hex code, U+ codepoint, Unicode general-category and block, buffer position; pure builder in zmax-core::cursor_info, unit tested

C-u: arms an emacs prefix argument for the next command - 4, and x4 per repeat (C-u C-u = 16); digits typed after it replace the number (C-u 3 0 = 30). A positive argument becomes the command's count, so C-u 5 C-f moves five characters; commands that branch on the argument's shape read Context::prefix_arg(). Distinct from the vim count: it can be negative, and a bare C-u is distinguishable from an explicit 4

M-0..M-9: numeric prefix argument. EditorView::handle_prefix_key folds the digit in at the alt-digit keys directly (guarded so it never steals a bound chord); by name it arms the argument and the digits typed next build it
emacs delete-duplicate-lines -> dedup (exact-name alias delete-duplicate-lines): keep first of each line, drop later duplicates.
emacs indent-rigidly
downcase-word (M-l): lower-case the word after point
transpose-sexps (C-M-t): swap the current s-expression with the previous one
transpose-paragraphs: swap the current paragraph with the previous one

vim visual-block (CTRL-V) is a true persistent rectangular mode, matching IntelliJ column-selection-mode

Complete Current Statement (Cmd-Shift-Enter) = complete_current_statement, bound under the Emacs major-mode prefix C-c as `C-c ;` — active in Insert, Normal AND Select (spacemacs), so it fires while typing like JetBrains. For a `fn`/`if`/`for`/`while`/`match`/`impl`/... header (line_opens_block) in a brace language (lang_uses_braces), closes any open `(`/`[` then inserts `{ body }` and drops the caret on the indented body line — e.g. `fn tommy()` -> `fn tommy() {<caret>}`, `while (1)` -> `while (1) {<caret>}`. Otherwise closes open brackets/parens, appends the terminator (`;` for semicolon languages via lang_uses_semicolons), and opens an indented line below. Brace vs semicolon are independent axes so brace-but-not-semicolon languages (Go, stryke) still get braces; an unknown/scratch buffer defaults to brace-capable (C-family). Core decision is the pure, unit-tested plan_complete_statement. Line-local heuristic (not full PSI).

C-c C-n moves to the next message with the same subject (rmail-next/previous-same-subject). Real two-key chord: C-c now arms rmail-mode's prefix (it used to quit; `q`/Esc quit). N/P stay bound as the aliases.

C-c C-p moves to the previous message with the same subject (rmail-next/previous-same-subject). Real two-key chord: C-c now arms rmail-mode's prefix (it used to quit; `q`/Esc quit). N/P stay bound as the aliases.
rmail-summary -> Rmail h lists every message in a summary pane
rmail-summary-quit -> q leaves the summary pane
rmail-summary-wipe -> Q leaves the summary pane
Rmail h (make summary)
Rmail summary Q (wipe)
Rmail summary q (quit)
rmail-add-label -> Rmail a (inline prompt, comma-separated labels)
rmail-kill-label -> Rmail k (inline prompt)
rmail-summary-by-labels -> Rmail l (prompt, filtered summary pane)
rmail-search -> Rmail M-s (inline prompt, forward substring search)
rmail-input -> Rmail i (prompt, load another mbox file)
rmail-output -> Rmail o (prompt, append current message to an mbox file)
rmail-output-body-to-file -> Rmail w (prompt, write body to a file)
rmail-output-as-seen -> Rmail O (append the message exactly as shown -- currently visible headers plus body -- to an mbox file)
rmail-sort-by-date -> Rmail M-d; stable-sorts the mailbox by a YYYYMMDDhhmmss key parsed from each Date header (pure zmax_core::rmail::sortable_date), cursor follows the current message
rmail-sort-by-subject -> Rmail M-j; sorts by subject with Re:/Fwd: stripped so replies group with their root (Msg::subject_key)
rmail-sort-by-author -> Rmail M-a; sorts by the From address with the display name stripped (mail-strip-quoted-names equivalent, Msg::author_key)
rmail-sort-by-recipient -> Rmail M-r; sorts by the first stripped To address (Msg::recipient_key)
rmail-sort-by-correspondent -> Rmail M-e; sorts by the correspondent address. zmax has no configured user identity, so it approximates Emacs (which skips self) with the first non-empty of From/To/Cc
rmail-sort-by-lines -> Rmail M-l; sorts by body line count (Msg::line_count)
rmail-sort-by-labels -> Rmail M-b; sorts by the message's sorted label string (Msg::labels_key)
rmail-summary-by-senders -> Rmail S; prompts for a regexp and opens a summary of the messages whose From matches (zmax_core::rmail::filter_by_senders)
rmail-summary-by-recipients -> Rmail T; opens a summary of messages whose To/From/Cc matches the given regexp (filter_by_recipients)
rmail-summary-by-topic -> Rmail B; opens a summary of messages whose Subject matches the given regexp (filter_by_topic)
rmail-summary-by-regexp -> Rmail G; opens a summary of messages whose whole text (all headers plus body) matches the regexp (filter_by_regexp)
rmail-next-labeled-message -> Rmail C-n; prompts for a label and moves forward to the next message carrying it (Mailbox::next_labeled)
rmail-previous-labeled-message -> Rmail C-p; moves back to the previous message carrying the given label (Mailbox::prev_labeled)
rmail-summary-undelete-many -> Rmail U; clears the Deleted flag on every deleted message (Mailbox::undelete_all). The optional numeric-prefix variant is not implemented
rmail-edit-current-message -> Rmail e; enters an inline edit mode over the current message body
rmail-cease-edit -> Rmail M-c while editing; commits the edited body back to the message
rmail-abort-edit -> Rmail M-k (or Esc) while editing; discards the edit and restores the stored body
now on Emacs's key `b` (z stays as the alias)
now on Emacs's key `b` in the summary pane

C-x r M: set a bookmark only if the name is unused (BookmarkStore::set_no_overwrite); errors on collision. New static command; verified it resolves.
Picker over bookmarks; selecting removes it from the store and re-persists. New static command; verified it resolves.

Jump to the [count]th quickfix error on a line above the cursor line in the current buffer; multi-error lines count as one stop, landing on the last entry of the target line (Vim :cabove). Engine: zmax_core::quickfix::above; wired via qf_move_dir.

Jump to the [count]th quickfix error on a line below the cursor line; multi-error lines count as one stop, landing on the first entry of the target line (Vim :cbelow). Engine: zmax_core::quickfix::below.
Jump to the [count]th quickfix error strictly before the cursor position, comparing line then column; every entry is its own stop (Vim :cbefore). Engine: zmax_core::quickfix::before.
Jump to the [count]th quickfix error strictly after the cursor position, comparing line then column (Vim :cafter). Engine: zmax_core::quickfix::after.
Location-list twin of :cabove, operating on the current window's loclist via qf_move_dir(QfKind::Location, Above).
Location-list twin of :cbelow via qf_move_dir(QfKind::Location, Below).
Location-list twin of :cbefore via qf_move_dir(QfKind::Location, Before).
Location-list twin of :cafter via qf_move_dir(QfKind::Location, After).

New :string-insert-rectangle typable inserts text at the rectangle's left column on each selected line without overwriting (shifts text right), via new pure zmax_core::text_engine::string_insert_rectangle. Verified name resolves in gen_port_report.parse_typable_commands().
defines an abbreviation (abbrev_define)
insert-mode abbreviation
command-line abbreviation; zmax has one abbreviation table
:unabbreviate removes an abbreviation for both modes; errors E24 when absent.
:iunabbreviate removes an Insert-mode abbreviation (also drops a both-mode entry active in insert).
:cunabbreviate removes a Command-line-mode abbreviation.
:abclear clears all abbreviations for both modes.
:iabclear clears all Insert-mode abbreviations (insert map + shared both map).
:cabclear clears all Command-line-mode abbreviations.
buffer-menu: static command pushes the Buffer Menu Component
Buffer-menu-mark -> m marks the buffer for display (> tag) and advances
Buffer-menu-unmark -> u clears the mark at point and advances
Buffer-menu-unmark-all -> M-DEL removes every mark of every kind
Buffer-menu-unmark-all-buffers -> U removes every mark
Buffer-menu-backup-unmark -> DEL moves up one line and clears its mark
Buffer-menu-delete -> d flags the buffer for deletion (D) and advances
Buffer-menu-delete-backwards -> C-d flags for deletion (D) and moves up
Buffer-menu-save -> s flags the buffer to be saved (S) and advances
Buffer-menu-not-modified -> ~ clears the buffer's modified (*) flag
Buffer-menu-toggle-read-only -> % toggles the buffer's read-only flag
Buffer-menu-execute -> x saves the S-flagged buffers then kills the D-flagged ones (real editor save/close)
Buffer-menu-select -> v selects the buffer at point plus every >-marked buffer, first in this window and the rest in splits
Buffer-menu-this-window -> RET/f selects the buffer at point in this window
buffer-menu-other-window -> o selects the buffer at point in another (split) window
Buffer-menu-other-window -> o selects the buffer at point in another (split) window
Buffer-menu-switch-other-window -> C-o displays the buffer at point in another window, staying in the menu
Buffer-menu-1-window -> 1 selects the buffer at point filling the frame (Replace)
now the real command: this line's buffer in one window, the previously-current buffer in the other
Buffer-menu-bury -> b sinks the buffer's row to the bottom of the menu list; zmax has no separate global buffer-list order, so the global other-buffer effect is not reproduced
Buffer-menu-toggle-files-only -> T lists only file-visiting buffers

Buffer-menu-toggle-internal -> I toggles showing internal (space-prefixed / *…* special) buffers; the filter is faithful but zmax does not create Emacs-style internal buffers, so in practice it reveals nothing
RET = Buffer-menu-this-window: select buffer at point (Editor::switch Replace)
f = Buffer-menu-this-window: select buffer at point
o = Buffer-menu-other-window: select in a split window
1 = Buffer-menu-1-window: select buffer filling the frame (Replace)

2 selects this line's buffer AND puts the buffer that was current before the menu opened in the other window (Buffer-menu-2-window), with point in this line's buffer. It used to be a plain other-window split.
v = Buffer-menu-select: select buffer at point plus all >-marked buffers
m = Buffer-menu-mark: mark > for selection and advance
d = Buffer-menu-delete: flag D for deletion and advance
C-d = Buffer-menu-delete-backwards: flag D and move up
s = Buffer-menu-save: flag S to be saved and advance
x = Buffer-menu-execute: save S buffers, then kill D buffers, then refresh
u = Buffer-menu-unmark: clear mark at point and advance
DEL (Backspace) = Buffer-menu-backup-unmark: move up and clear mark
U = Buffer-menu-unmark-all-buffers: remove every mark
M-DEL reads the mark character to remove and drops only that mark from every buffer — RET removes them all (Buffer-menu-unmark-all-buffers). `U` still clears everything unconditionally.
~ = Buffer-menu-not-modified: clear the modified flag (reset_modified)
% = Buffer-menu-toggle-read-only: toggle the buffer's read-only flag
T = Buffer-menu-toggle-files-only: list only file-visiting buffers
q = quit-window: close the Buffer Menu overlay

New pure zmax-core::whitespace::tabify (faithful tabify.el: runs of 2+ blanks replaced via indent-to preserving end column) wired to typable :tabify over the primary selection's line range at buffer tab_width. Unit-tested incl round-trips.
occur-mode-goto-occurrence: RET pops the overlay and moves point to the occurrence in the source view.
occur-mode-goto-occurrence-other-window: o goes to the occurrence in the source window (overlay model pops + jumps).
occur-mode-display-occurrence: C-o sets the source selection to the occurrence while leaving the *Occur* list open.
emacs compile-goto-error
New. Sorts selected lines alphabetically by the character-column range [beg,end) via zmax_core::sort::sort_columns; --reverse for descending. Tabs treated as single columns (Emacs rejects tabs).
ispell (M-x ispell): spell-check the region if selected else the whole buffer via the external speller
ispell-region: spell-check the selection with aspell/hunspell/ispell -a; reports count and jumps to the first misspelling
ispell-buffer: spell-check the whole buffer with the external speller
ispell-change-dictionary: prompt for the -d dictionary/language passed to the speller
diary: display today's diary entries (reads ~/diary via zmax_core::diary)
diary-view-entries: show the current date's entries (calendar `d` shows the date at point)
diary-show-all-entries: open the ~/diary file
diary-insert-entry: append a dated header for today to ~/diary and open it to type
diary-insert-weekly-entry: append a weekly (weekday) header for today
diary-mark-entries: the Calendar marks dates carrying entries; the command reports the marked-date count for the month
d (Calendar mode) = diary-view-entries: show the diary entries for the date at point
outline-mode: next heading (outline-next-visible-heading, C-c C-n); heading structure from zmax_core::outline, folding via the document fold state
outline-mode: previous heading (outline-previous-visible-heading, C-c C-p); heading structure from zmax_core::outline, folding via the document fold state
outline-mode: parent heading (outline-up-heading, C-c C-u); heading structure from zmax_core::outline, folding via the document fold state
outline-mode: next same-level heading (outline-forward-same-level, C-c C-f); heading structure from zmax_core::outline, folding via the document fold state
outline-mode: previous same-level heading (outline-backward-same-level, C-c C-b); heading structure from zmax_core::outline, folding via the document fold state
outline-mode: fold the subtree body (outline-hide-subtree); heading structure from zmax_core::outline, folding via the document fold state
outline-mode: reveal the subtree (outline-show-subtree); heading structure from zmax_core::outline, folding via the document fold state
outline-mode: fold this heading's body (outline-hide-entry); heading structure from zmax_core::outline, folding via the document fold state
outline-mode: reveal this heading's body (outline-show-entry); heading structure from zmax_core::outline, folding via the document fold state
outline-mode: fold all bodies, show only headings (outline-hide-body); heading structure from zmax_core::outline, folding via the document fold state
outline-mode: reveal all body text (outline-show-all); heading structure from zmax_core::outline, folding via the document fold state

outline-mode: show only the top N levels of headings, hiding bodies and deeper headings (outline-hide-sublevels / hide-sublevels); zmax_core::outline::sublevel_folds, folding via the document fold state
outline-mode: fold bodies within the current subtree, keeping subheadings visible (outline-hide-leaves); zmax_core::outline::subtree_leaf_bodies, folding via the document fold state
outline-mode: reveal only the immediate subheadings of the heading at point (outline-show-children); zmax_core::outline::subtree_child_folds, folding via the document fold state
outline-mode: reveal every subheading in the subtree at point (outline-show-branches); zmax_core::outline::subtree_leaf_bodies, folding via the document fold state

outline-mode: org-style TAB cycling of the heading at point folded -> children -> subtree, chosen from how much of the subtree is hidden (zmax_core::outline::outline_cycle_next); folding via the document fold state

outline-mode: org global TAB cycling of the whole buffer show-all -> overview (top headings) -> contents (all headings), chosen from the current fold state (zmax_core::outline::outline_cycle_buffer_next); reuses sublevel_folds/all_bodies
persistent hi-lock highlight; prompts for the regexp when given none
hi-lock phrase (whitespace collapsed to \s+); prompts when given none
whole-line hi-lock; prompts when given none
emacs unhighlight-regexp -> :unhighlight-regexp [<regex>]: remove one Hi-Lock highlight, or all when no argument is given
emacs highlight-symbol-at-point (M-s h .) -> :highlight-symbol-at-point: Hi-Lock the symbol under the cursor as a whole-word regexp (crate::hi_lock::symbol_regexp + add)
emacs hi-lock-write-interactive-patterns -> :hi-lock-write-interactive-patterns: insert active Hi-Lock patterns at point as commented Hi-lock: font-lock-keyword lines
emacs hi-lock-find-patterns -> :hi-lock-find-patterns: scan the first 10000 bytes for commented Hi-lock: lines and activate their highlights
outline-mode: fold the subtree of every heading whose line matches the regexp (outline-hide-by-heading-regexp); zmax_core::outline::matching_subtree_bodies, folding via the document fold state
outline-mode: reveal the subtree of every heading whose line matches the regexp, opening any folds that hid it (outline-show-by-heading-regexp); folding via the document fold state
emacs write-region -> :write-region <path>: write the region (or buffer) to the named file, replacing it
emacs append-to-file -> :append-to-file <path>: append the region (or buffer) to the end of the named file
emacs insert-abbrevs -> insert_abbrevs: insert a description of every defined abbrev at point (emacs_abbrev::serialize)
emacs define-abbrevs -> define_abbrevs: define abbrevs from the buffer text after point (emacs_abbrev::define_from_text)
emacs write-abbrev-file -> :write-abbrev-file <path>: export every abbrev to the named file in the store's name\texpansion format (emacs_abbrev::write_to)
emacs read-abbrev-file -> :read-abbrev-file <path>: read abbrevs from the named file and merge them into the table (emacs_abbrev::read_from)

emacs set-fill-column (C-x f) -> :set-fill-column [N]: set the fill width to N or the current cursor column; applies to the text-width config (zmax's fill-width model, same value :set tw sets, drives :reflow)
emacs bookmark-write -> :bookmark-write <path>: export every bookmark to the named file in the store text format (emacs_bookmark::write_to)
emacs bookmark-load -> :bookmark-load <path>: read bookmarks from the named file and merge them into the store (BookmarkStore::merge, emacs_bookmark::load_from)
emacs bookmark-insert-location -> bookmark_insert_location: pick a bookmark and insert its file path at point
emacs bookmark-insert -> bookmark_insert: pick a bookmark and insert the contents of the file it points to at point
emacs copy-rectangle-to-register -> copy_rectangle_to_register: store the selected rectangle in a register (RegVal::Rect); insert-register (C-x r i) yanks it back as a rectangle
calendar-forward-year: `]` moves the point date forward a year (emacs C-x ])
calendar-backward-year: `[` moves the point date back a year (emacs C-x [)
calendar-beginning-of-month: `{` moves to the first of the month
calendar-end-of-month: `}` moves to the last day of the month
calendar-beginning-of-year: `(` moves to January 1
calendar-end-of-year: `)` moves to December 31
re-keyed: the ISO date is now `p c`; `i` is the diary-insert prefix
calendar-julian-print-date: Julian (Roman) date via fixed_from_julian/julian_from_fixed; matches Emacs (2000-01-01 -> Dec 19 1999)
now `p d` (Emacs's chord), under the real `p` print prefix
:spellwrong -> zmax :spellwrong marks words bad via crate::spell::add_bad (like zw)
:spellundo -> zmax :spellundo removes words from the good/bad lists (zug/zuw)
:spelldump -> zmax :spelldump opens a buffer of the user's known-good words
:spellinfo -> zmax :spellinfo reports the wordlist location and sizes
adds the word to the spell file as rare
align-current: auto-detect the region's dominant delimiter and align into columns, sections split at blank lines (zmax_core::power_edit::align_current)
align-entire: align the whole region as one section (zmax_core::power_edit::align_entire)
copy-to-register (C-x r s): store the region in a prompted register, replacing its contents
append-to-register (C-x r a): add the region after a register's existing text
prepend-to-register (C-x r p): add the region before a register's existing text
kmacro-set-counter: set the keyboard-macro counter to the numeric prefix (absolute)
kmacro-set-format: set the printf format the counter is inserted with (%d, %03d, 0x%x)
view-file (C-x C-r): open a file read-only for viewing (sets the buffer read-only)
view-buffer: make the current buffer read-only for viewing
list-abbrevs: show all defined abbreviations in a buffer
define-global-abbrev NAME EXPANSION: add a global abbreviation to the table
kill-all-abbrevs: remove every defined abbreviation
tab-select: switch to the [count]-th tab via Editor::switch_tab
tab-recent: switch to the most recently visited tab (tracked across the tab-navigation commands)
forward-list (C-M-n): move forward over a balanced () group
backward-list (C-M-p): move backward over a balanced () group
down-list (C-M-d): descend into the next list past its open
kill-sexp (C-M-k): kill the s-expression after point (list or atom)
mark-sexp (C-M-SPC): set the region over the following s-expression
kill-sentence (M-k): kill from point to end of sentence, saved to the kill ring
backward-kill-sentence (C-x DEL): kill from start of sentence to point
forward-page (C-x ]): move to the next form-feed page
backward-page (C-x [): move to the previous form-feed page
mark-page (C-x C-p): select the current form-feed page
copy-matching-lines: copy lines matching a regex to the kill ring
scroll-other-window (C-M-v): scroll the other window forward
scroll-other-window-down (C-M-S-v): scroll the other window backward
not-modified (M-~): mark the buffer unmodified without saving
backward-delete-char-untabify: delete backward, expanding a tab into spaces first
add-name-to-file -> :add-name-to-file <newname>: create a hard link to the current buffer's visited file
make-symbolic-link -> :make-symbolic-link <target> <linkname> (or <linkname> for the current file): create a symbolic link

fill-individual-paragraphs -> :fill-individual-paragraphs [width]: fill each paragraph of the region separately, splitting on indentation changes, each paragraph filled under its own indentation as fill prefix

fill-nonuniform-paragraphs -> :fill-nonuniform-paragraphs [width]: fill each paragraph of the region separately, splitting only on blank lines, using the smallest indentation of each paragraph as fill prefix
change buffer's visited file
chmod a file, octal mode
recursive directory copy
insert raw file at point
directory listing in scratch buffer
open a URL in the OS browser
goto line relative to narrowed region
report editor version
report an environment variable
set/unset an environment variable
list commands matching a regexp
expand all abbrevs in region
run the input line
previous input (M-p)
next input (M-n)
run gdb in a comint buffer
stack frame up
stack frame down
step one instruction
temporary breakpoint at current line
print expr at point
watch expr at point
flag backup (~) files for deletion
flag auto-save (#name#) files
re-keyed: flagging the garbage files is now `% &` (Emacs's key); bare `&` is dired-do-async-shell-command
redisplay/refresh the listing
move to next marked file
move to previous marked file
toggle hide-details (name-only rows)
scroll calendar 3 months forward
scroll calendar 3 months back
outline visibility cycling on org heading
cycle TODO/DONE keyword
open the org agenda view
emacs align
diff buffer vs git HEAD (not two arbitrary files)
set-justification-left -> :set-justification-left: flush the region's lines to the left margin (strip surrounding whitespace)
set-justification-right -> :set-justification-right: right-justify each of the region's lines to the fill width
set-justification-center -> :set-justification-center: centre the region's lines within the fill width
set-justification-full -> :set-justification-full: justify the region's lines to both margins by stretching inter-word gaps (each paragraph's last line left flush)

set-justification-none -> :set-justification-none: one-shot region transform trims trailing whitespace to turn justification off; does not restore the persistent enriched-mode justification text property
set-left-margin -> :set-left-margin [col]: rewrite each region content line so its left margin is exactly col spaces (default 0)
increase-left-margin -> :increase-left-margin [n]: indent each region content line by n columns (default standard-indent 4); :decrease-left-margin is the inverse

append-to-buffer -> :append-to-buffer <buffer>: copy the region to the end of another open buffer (matched by name or basename) as one undo step; requires the target buffer to already be open (does not auto-create it)
prepend-to-buffer -> :prepend-to-buffer <buffer>: copy the region to the beginning of another open buffer; :copy-to-buffer replaces the target's whole contents with the region
copy-to-buffer -> :copy-to-buffer <buffer>: replace another open buffer's entire contents with the region
renames the buffer; prompts when given no name
rename-uniquely -> :rename-uniquely: rename the current buffer to a similar name carrying the lowest free <N> numeric suffix (strips any existing suffix first)

desktop-save -> :desktop-save [DIR]: record every file-visiting buffer plus its point into DIR/.zmax.desktop (DIR defaults to the last desktop dir, else ~); the current buffer is flagged so a later read restores focus to it
desktop-read -> :desktop-read [DIR]: reopen every file listed in DIR/.zmax.desktop, restoring focus and point to the buffer flagged current; missing files are skipped
desktop-change-dir -> :desktop-change-dir DIR: switch to the desktop saved in DIR, reading its recorded buffer set
desktop-revert -> :desktop-revert: re-read the last loaded desktop, discarding edits to the recorded buffer set
desktop-clear -> :desktop-clear: close every open buffer and erase the desktop file in the active directory (modified buffers block the close and are reported)
apropos -> :apropos: list commands (by name or doc) and config variables matching a regexp
apropos-documentation -> :apropos-documentation: list commands whose doc string matches a regexp
apropos-variable -> :apropos-variable: list config variables by name with their current value
apropos-user-option -> :apropos-user-option: list user-customizable options (zmax config is all user-facing)
apropos-value -> :apropos-value: list config variables whose current value matches a regexp
Move to start of the on-screen (visual) line; soft-wrap aware, degenerates to logical line start when wrapping is off.
Move to end of the on-screen (visual) line; soft-wrap aware.
Move down one logical line ignoring soft-wrap = move_line_down (move_vertically).
Move up one logical line ignoring soft-wrap = move_line_up (move_vertically).
Split window + go to next buffer; reuses :bnext navigation.
Split window + go to previous buffer; reuses :bprev navigation.
:sbNext is an alias of :sbprevious (split + previous buffer).
Split window + go to first buffer in the list.
:sbrewind is an alias of :sbfirst (split + first buffer).
Split window + go to last buffer in the list.
Split window + go to next modified buffer; reuses :bmodified logic.
Split window + go to buffer whose path contains {name}; reuses :buffer selection.
:sball opens a window per buffer = existing :ball (alias sball).

partial: zmax config variables are global, not buffer-local; apropos over the editor config variables
partial: zmax config variables are global, not buffer-local; apropos over the editor config variables
emacs hl-line-mode -> toggle_hl_line: flips config.cursorline, which the renderer (ui/editor.rs) uses to highlight the current line. zmax cursorline is editor-global.
emacs global-hl-line-mode -> toggle_hl_line: same editor-global cursorline highlight as hl-line-mode (zmax has no per-buffer/global split).
emacs electric-pair-mode -> toggle_electric_pair: flips config.auto_pairs Enable(true/false); refresh_config rebuilds live Editor.auto_pairs so insert_char auto-inserts matching delimiters.
emacs auto-revert-mode -> toggle_auto_revert: flips config.auto_reload (vim autoread), which re-reads unmodified buffers when the file changes on disk. Editor-global.
emacs global-auto-revert-mode -> toggle_auto_revert: same editor-global auto_reload toggle as auto-revert-mode.
emacs global-whitespace-mode -> toggle_whitespace_render: renders whitespace glyphs (spaces/tabs) via config.whitespace.render. zmax render is editor-global.

emacs set-fill-prefix (C-x .) -> set_fill_prefix: stores text from line start to point in Editor.fill_prefix; auto_fill_after_insert prepends it to each wrapped continuation line. Empty prefix clears it.
emacs set-goal-column (C-x C-n) -> set_goal_column: stores current column in Editor.goal_column; move_line_up/down then snap the cursor to that column (clamped to line length). Re-invoking clears it.
toggles the fill-column ruler for every window
emacs global-eldoc-mode -> alias of eldoc-mode (ex_eldoc_mode) toggling lsp.auto-signature-help: automatic display of signature/parameter hints at point, the zmax analogue of eldoc's echo-area docs.
Kills the comment on the current line (count kills that many lines) to the kill ring = emacs comment-kill; tree-sitter comment node with string-aware line-comment-token fallback.

abbrev-prefix-mark (M-'): mark point as the start of an abbrev so the next expand-abbrev treats only the text after the mark as the abbrev, keeping any non-abbrev prefix. Faithful to lisp/abbrev.el: without a prefix arg it first expands any abbrev before point ((or arg (expand-abbrev))), then records the location (abbrev-start-location / ABBREV_START keyed on DocumentId) and inserts a hyphen ((insert "-")). expand_abbrev, refactored into abbrev_expand_impl, consults the location via take_abbrev_start and abbrev--before-point's logic: strip the hyphen, take text up to point minus trailing whitespace (abbrev_prefix_split) as the abbrev name, expand it, and remove a dangling hyphen when nothing matches. Bound at SPC x e m.

unexpand-abbrev: undo the last expand-abbrev, replacing the inserted expansion with the original abbrev text and moving point past it, then clearing the saved text so a repeat is a no-op. Faithful to lisp/abbrev.el (last-abbrev / last-abbrev-text / last-abbrev-location recorded by expand_abbrev; unexpand_span helper for the replace span + point-max guard). Bound at SPC x e u.
inverse-add-global-abbrev: word before point becomes the abbrev name, prompt for expansion, define in the global table (reuses emacs_abbrev::define + shared abbrev_word_before helper)
kmacro-end-or-call-macro (C-x e / F4): ends the macro if recording, else replays the last macro; bound to F4.
append-next-kill (C-M-w): the next kill joins the last kill-ring entry instead of starting a new one (appended for a forward kill, prepended for a backward kill via record_prepend); bound at SPC r a.
comint-kill-input (C-c C-u): clears the pending input line via Comint::kill_input
comint-bol-or-process-mark (C-a): move to process mark / bol, via unit-tested bol_or_process_mark_target
comint-delchar-or-maybe-eof (C-d): delete char, or close child stdin (EOF) on empty input
comint-magic-space: expand !!/!$/!*/!-N/!str history designators (unit-tested expand_history) then insert a space
comint-insert-previous-argument (M-.): insert last arg (!$) of previous command via unit-tested last_argument
emacs comint-get-next-from-history
emacs comint-next-prompt
emacs comint-previous-prompt
comint-show-output (C-c C-r): scrolls the start of the last command's output to the top, via unit-tested last_prompt_line
comint-show-maximum-output (C-c C-e): scrolls newest output to the bottom
comint-delete-output (C-c C-o): deletes the last command's output lines via unit-tested last_output_range
comint-write-output: writes the last command's output (last_output_range) to a prompted file
emacs comint-copy-old-input
comint-truncate-buffer: trims scrollback to comint-buffer-maximum-size (1024) keeping the newest lines
comint-strip-ctrl-m: strips ^M (\r) from scrollback lines via unit-tested strip_ctrl_m
comint-interrupt-subjob (C-c C-c): sends real SIGINT to the child via libc::kill(child.id()) on unix; single direct child (no PTY process group)
comint-stop-subjob (C-c C-z): sends real SIGTSTP to the child via libc::kill on unix
comint-continue-subjob: sends real SIGCONT to the child via libc::kill on unix
comint-quit-subjob: sends real SIGQUIT to the child via libc::kill on unix
comint-dynamic-list-input-ring: lists the input-ring history inline in the buffer (Emacs pops a *Input History* buffer)
emacs comint-history-isearch-backward-regexp
comint-run: prompts for a program+args and opens it in a new comint buffer (Comint::with_program)
shell-forward-command (M-f): move point forward over a ;|& -separated shell command on the input line, via unit-tested forward_command
shell-backward-command (M-b): move point backward to the start of the shell command, via unit-tested backward_command
gud-jump: sends 'jump FILE:LINE' to the inferior debugger running in the comint buffer
emacs gud-refresh
Pure diffmode::diff_hunk_kill deletes the hunk at point; if it is the file's only hunk the file header is removed too (matches diff-remove-hunk). Char-for-char unit-tested.
Pure diffmode::diff_file_kill deletes the whole file section (banner + ---/+++ headers + hunks) at point. Unit-tested.
Pure diffmode::diff_split_hunk splits a unified hunk at point; recomputes correct start+length for BOTH halves (stock Emacs leaves the 2nd header at a ,1 placeholder). Unit-tested.
Pure diffmode::diff_reverse_direction swaps ---/+++ paths, each @@ range and every +/- body sign; reverse-then-reverse round-trips byte-for-byte (tested).

emacs diff-context->unified: pure diffmode::diff_context_to_unified converts a context diff to unified, interleaving both sides (reads ! change lines). Round-trip tested against diff-unified->context.
emacs diff-unified->context: pure diffmode::diff_unified_to_context converts unified to context (***/--- blocks, empty side omitted). Output matches diff -c; char-for-char tested.
emacs diff-delete-trailing-whitespace
Narrows the buffer to the hunk at point via diffmode::hunk_line_bounds + doc.narrow_to (widen to undo). The prefix-arg file variant is not wired.

Applies the hunk at point to its target file on disk: locates the old image at old_start (fallback whole-file scan), replaces with the new image, writes the file. Pure apply_hunk unit-tested (exact position, scan, and mismatch-error cases).
Applies every hunk of every file in the diff buffer to its target file (hunks applied last-to-first so line numbers stay valid). Pure apply_file_diff unit-tested.

Prompts for a name, stores name->keys in a process-global registry (zmax_core::kmacro), and makes the name resolve through MappableCommand::from_str so it can be bound to a key and replayed. Not shown in the command palette because zmax macro replay must be triggered from the main key loop (deferred callbacks are dropped in the palette).

Inserts a textual (fset 'last-kbd-macro (kmacro "KEYS")) definition of the last macro at point via zmax_core::kmacro::format_kbd_macro_definition. KEYS is zmax's key-string (round-trips through parse_macro), not Emacs format-kbd-macro spelling; inserts the last macro rather than prompting for a name.

Runs the last kbd macro once at the start of each line in the primary selection, feeding keys through the compositor. Recomputes each line start from line number rather than using Emacs markers, so a macro that changes the line count can drift; in-place per-line edits (the common case) work.
emacs kmacro-end-or-call-macro-repeat

Opens the last macro's key-string in a minibuffer prompt; on accept it re-parses and stores the edited keys as the last macro. Uses a one-line minibuffer editor, not Emacs's dedicated *Edit Macro* buffer with its annotated per-key layout.

emacs kmacro-step-edit-macro

reports the contents and WxH of the cell the caret sits in

cycles the current column's justification left->center->right->full (Justify + justify_line, unit tested); dense grid stores justification only at render time so only the most-recently-justified column keeps its alignment
raises the current column's minimum width by 1 (Table::widen_cell, unit tested); operates on the whole column since a dense grid shares one width per column
lowers the current column's minimum width by 1, never below the content width (Table::narrow_cell, unit tested)
raises the current row's minimum height by 1 line (Table::heighten_cell, unit tested); operates on the whole row (dense grid)
lowers the current row's minimum height by 1 line, never below 1 (Table::shorten_cell, unit tested)

UPGRADED from partial. The old note claimed there was no persistent line hi-lock and the command listed matches via occur instead - but hi_lock::add(src, whole_line) has a whole-line mode and its patterns are painted every frame (ui/editor.rs). It now installs a real persistent hi-lock pattern: every line containing the search stays highlighted end to end after the search ends, and unhighlight-regexp removes it. (isearch-highlight-regexp carried the same stale claim and was fixed with it)

default-indent-new-line (M-j): break line at point and continue the comment, aligning the continuation under the current comment (comment-indent-new-line in-comment path); newline-and-indent when not in a comment.

recenter-other-window (C-M-S-l): recenter point in the other window, equivalent to C-l (recenter) acting on the other window. Ports lisp/window.el, which runs recenter-top-bottom in other-window-for-scrolling; centers the other view via align_view, restoring focus.
Move to the start of the function at point via the tree-sitter `function` text object (like mark-defun's Around object).
Move to the end of the function at point via the tree-sitter `function` text object.
Select the whole function around point via the tree-sitter `function` text object.

Move to the first token of the C statement at point; pure `;`/`{`/`}`-delimited scan in zmax_core::cmode, unit tested. Does not parse string/comment contents (documented limitation matching the common case).
Move just past the next `;`/`{`/`}` ending the statement at point; pure scan in zmax_core::cmode, unit tested (same string/comment limitation).
Move forward across the following #if/#endif preprocessor conditional; depth-counting port of c-scan-conditionals core in zmax_core::cmode, unit tested (nested + enclosing cases).
Move backward across the preceding preprocessor conditional; mirror depth-counter in zmax_core::cmode, unit tested.
Move up out of the containing preprocessor conditional (skips balanced siblings, honours a numeric prefix for levels); pure up_conditional in zmax_core::cmode, unit tested.
Re-indent the current line or every selected line to the tree-sitter indentation (reuses zmax_core::indent::indent_for_newline per line).
Re-indent every line of the function at point (tree-sitter `function` object bounds + per-line tree-sitter reindent).

Align trailing backslash line-continuations in the region to a common column (c-backslash-column 48 / max 72), or remove them with a numeric prefix; pure align/remove/column helpers in zmax_core::cmode, unit tested.

Break the line at point: continue a // or * comment prefix, or append a `\` continuation inside a multi-line macro, else a plain indented newline; pure prefix/in_cpp_macro helpers in zmax_core::cmode, unit tested.

customize-option -> :customize-option [name]: open Settings pre-filtered to the named option (alias of :customize-variable).
customize-browse -> :customize-browse: open the Settings tab to browse all editor options (alias of :customize).
customize-face -> :customize-face: open the Color Scheme tab (theme/face editor) where face fg/bg/modifiers are edited live and saved.
customize-create-theme -> :customize-create-theme: open the Color Scheme tab, which builds and saves a custom theme from per-scope colors (alias of :customize-face).

set-selective-display (C-x $) -> hide lines indented past the numeric prefix column (only lines with < N columns of indentation stay visible), implemented over the fold model; no prefix disables it. Pure geometry in zmax_core::selective_display (unit-tested).

set-face-foreground -> :set-face-foreground <face> <color>: override a theme face's foreground live (hex or named color), applied via Theme::set_style + set_theme; affects UI chrome and syntax scopes.
set-face-background -> :set-face-background <face> <color>: override a theme face's background live, applied via Theme::set_style + set_theme.
global-whitespace-toggle-options -> toggle rendering of whitespace characters (whitespace.render All<->None) live in every buffer.
global-tab-line-mode -> toggle the buffer tab line (zmax bufferline Always<->Never) live.

set-right-margin -> :set-right-margin [n]: zmax has no persistent right-margin text property, so it re-fills the region to n columns narrower than text-width (the observable effect of a right margin on refill), reusing fill_paragraph.

toggle_superword toggles super-word w/b/e motions: symbol-syntax characters (_, -, etc.) count as part of a word so a whole snake_case_symbol moves as one word, the inverse of subword-mode. vim '' jumps to the first non-blank of the line the cursor was on before the latest jump. push_jump records the ` / ' context marks at the pre-jump position; '{mark} resolves to that line's first non-blank.
vim := echoes the last line number of the buffer (its line count) to the status line; empty buffer reports 1. Registered as :print-line-number with := alias (mirrors :number/:# naming).
vim :smagic forces 'magic' on the substitute pattern (prefixes \m). Both :smagic/p/r/f (no-space, parsed in execute_command_line, supports %) and :smagic /p/r/f (space form) work.
vim :snomagic forces 'nomagic' (pattern literal except anchors; prefixes \M). Both :snomagic/p/r/f (no-space, supports %) and the space form work.
vim :dl = :delete with the l (list) flag: deletes the current line(s) into the unnamed register and echoes the last deleted line in :list format ($ marks the line end).
vim :changes opens the buffer changelist picker (real typable, build_changelist_picker), or reports "No changes" when empty; the analog of :jumps.

vim :iput / :ip puts a register below the cursor and re-indents the block so its first line matches the current line's leading whitespace (later lines shift by the same delta, blank lines stay empty).

vim :@{reg} executes a register as Ex command line(s) (each register newline runs as its own command; :@: runs the last command line). Registered as :execute-register with a :@ alias; the no-space :@a form is parsed in execute_command_line.
vim :@@ repeats the last :@{reg} (the register last executed by :@ / :execute-register, tracked in a thread-local).
jumps to the identifier's definition (goto_ident_match)
jumps to the #define of the identifier (goto_ident_match)
splits the window and jumps to the identifier
splits the window and jumps to the #define
lists the first line the identifier matches
lists the first #define line of the macro

vim :{range} with no command moves the cursor to the last line of the range (:2,4 -> line 4, :$, :.+3, :'<,'>), landing on its first non-blank; guarded so the shift commands :> / :< are not swallowed.
vim :echoerr / :echoe prints its arguments to the statusline as an error (mirrors :echo but via set_error).
lists the lines the identifier matches
lists the #define lines of the macro
lists the digraph table in a scratch buffer
prints a window of lines from the cursor into a scratch buffer
resolves each 'include' line through 'includeexpr' and the 'path' search and lists the ones not found; :checkpath! lists all (checkpath_impl, typed.rs:46098)
lists the Ex commands in a scratch buffer
lists the normal-mode commands in a scratch buffer
vim :balt {file} adds the file to the buffer list (like :badd) and sets it as the alternate file (the CTRL-^ / `` target) without switching to it.
changes the working directory; zmax has one global cwd
fires an autocmd event for every loaded buffer
vim :lolder / :lol goes to an older location list in the current window's location-list history (per-window stack, mirrors the quickfix :colder history).
vim :lnewer / :lnew goes to a newer location list in the window's location-list history.
vim :lhistory / :lhi shows the position in the window's location-list history.
vim :lnfile jumps to the first location-list entry in the next file (QfKind::Location variant of :cnfile).
vim :lNfile jumps to the last location-list entry in the previous file (QfKind::Location variant of :cpfile).
sets the argument list; zmax has one global list
sets the argument list; zmax has one global list
vim CTRL-W CTRL-_ (same as CTRL-W _) maximizes the window (wonly), aliased alongside C-w _.
CTRL-W T -> window_to_new_tab: move current window to a new tabpage (fails if sole window)
:diffoff -> zmax :diffoff removes the side-by-side diff overlay (turns diff mode off)
:unhide -> zmax :unhide (alias of :ball; every zmax buffer is loaded so this opens a window per buffer)
:sunhide -> zmax :sunhide (alias of :ball; splits a window per loaded buffer)
:match {group} /pat/ -> zmax :match, group slot 1 on the Hi-Lock overlay; :match none clears (colour by index, not by named group)
:2match {group} /pat/ -> zmax :2match, independent group slot 2 on the Hi-Lock overlay; :2match none clears
:3match {group} /pat/ -> zmax :3match, independent group slot 3 on the Hi-Lock overlay; :3match none clears
scans a directory's vim-format *.txt help files for *tag* markers and writes a `tags` file (ex_helptags, typed.rs:23012); was a status message only
:sign define/undefine/place/unplace/list/jump -> zmax :sign; real substrate: zmax_view::signs SignStore + GutterType::Signs sign column
:undojoin -> zmax :undojoin; real substrate: History::merge_last_revision composes the next commit into the current revision so one undo reverts both. Document.undojoin_pending flag.

emacs define-mode-abbrev NAME EXPANSION -> :define-mode-abbrev; stores in the current buffer's major-mode (language) abbrev table via emacs_abbrev::define_mode; expand-abbrev searches it before global
emacs add-mode-abbrev (C-x a l) -> add_mode_abbrev: prompt name+expansion, define in the current major-mode-local abbrev table
emacs inverse-add-mode-abbrev (C-x a i l) -> inverse_add_mode_abbrev: word before point becomes the name, prompt for expansion, define mode-local

emacs abbrev-mode minor mode -> toggle_abbrev_mode / :abbrev-mode; Editor.abbrev_mode flag drives an insert-path hook: typing a non-word char auto-expands the abbrev before point (expand-abbrev), mirroring self-insert-command
:append -> zmax :append opens the ExInput line-input mode (ui/ex_input.rs): types lines after the current line, ends on a lone '.'
:insert -> zmax :insert (ExInput): types lines before the current line, ends on a lone '.'
:change -> zmax :change (ExInput): replaces the current line with typed lines, ends on a lone '.'
emacs timeclock-in -> timeclock_in: prompt for project, clock in (guards against double clock-in). Pure model zmax-core/src/timeclock.rs, file-backed timelog
emacs timeclock-out -> timeclock_out: clock out (guards against not-clocked-in)
emacs timeclock-change -> timeclock_change: clock out then prompt+clock in to a new project
emacs timeclock-workday-remaining -> timeclock_workday_remaining: workday (8h) minus time worked today (UTC day)
emacs timeclock-when-to-leave -> timeclock_when_to_leave: shows remaining time (relative, no tz assumptions)
emacs timeclock-reread-log -> timeclock_reread_log: reload the timelog and report clock state
emacs timeclock-mode-line-display -> timeclock_mode_line_display: echo today's worked/remaining time and clock state
emacs tab-bar-mode -> tab_bar_mode: toggle the tab bar (zmax bufferline) via edit_live_config
emacs tab-rename -> tab_rename (prompt) / :tab-rename (arg); TabPage.name field + Editor::rename_current_tab
emacs tab-switch -> tab_switch (prompt) / :tab-switch (arg): switch by tab name or 1-based number via Editor::tab_names
emacs tab-undo -> tab_undo / :tab-undo: reopen most recently closed tab (Editor.closed_tabs stack + reopen_closed_tab)
emacs tab-bar-history-mode -> tab_bar_history_mode: toggle Editor.tab_history_mode (records tab-visit history in switch_tab)
emacs tab-bar-history-back -> tab_bar_history_back / :tab-bar-history-back: Editor.tab_history_back (tab_back/tab_forward stacks)
emacs tab-bar-history-forward -> tab_bar_history_forward / :tab-bar-history-forward: Editor.tab_history_forward
( toggles hide-details (dired-hide-details-mode) — same key

% & flags the garbage files (build + TeX droppings). Real two-key chord: `%` arms a prefix (ui/dired.rs Prefix::Pct) and the next key runs it; the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

% C copies each target through a regexp -> replacement. Real two-key chord: `%` arms a prefix (ui/dired.rs Prefix::Pct) and the next key runs it; the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.
% d flags for deletion by name regexp. Real two-key chord: `%` arms a prefix (ui/dired.rs Prefix::Pct) and the next key runs it; the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.
% g marks files whose CONTENTS match a regexp. Real two-key chord: `%` arms a prefix (ui/dired.rs Prefix::Pct) and the next key runs it; the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

% H hardlinks each target through a regexp -> replacement. Real two-key chord: `%` arms a prefix (ui/dired.rs Prefix::Pct) and the next key runs it; the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.
% l downcases the names on disk. Real two-key chord: `%` arms a prefix (ui/dired.rs Prefix::Pct) and the next key runs it; the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.
% m marks by name regexp. Real two-key chord: `%` arms a prefix (ui/dired.rs Prefix::Pct) and the next key runs it; the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

% R renames each target through a regexp -> replacement. Real two-key chord: `%` arms a prefix (ui/dired.rs Prefix::Pct) and the next key runs it; the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

% S symlinks each target through a regexp -> replacement. Real two-key chord: `%` arms a prefix (ui/dired.rs Prefix::Pct) and the next key runs it; the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.
% u upcases the names on disk. Real two-key chord: `%` arms a prefix (ui/dired.rs Prefix::Pct) and the next key runs it; the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

% Y relsymlinks each target through a regexp -> replacement. Real two-key chord: `%` arms a prefix (ui/dired.rs Prefix::Pct) and the next key runs it; the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.
* ! drops every mark (dired-unmark-all-marks). Real two-key chord: `*` arms a prefix (ui/dired.rs Prefix::Star); the parser can only express the prefix key.
* % marks by name regexp. Real two-key chord: `*` arms a prefix (ui/dired.rs Prefix::Star); the parser can only express the prefix key.
* * marks the executables. Real two-key chord: `*` arms a prefix (ui/dired.rs Prefix::Star); the parser can only express the prefix key.
* / marks the directories. Real two-key chord: `*` arms a prefix (ui/dired.rs Prefix::Star); the parser can only express the prefix key.
* @ marks the symlinks. Real two-key chord: `*` arms a prefix (ui/dired.rs Prefix::Star); the parser can only express the prefix key.
* c changes one mark character into another. Real two-key chord: `*` arms a prefix (ui/dired.rs Prefix::Star); the parser can only express the prefix key.
* C-n moves to the next marked file. Real two-key chord: `*` arms a prefix (ui/dired.rs Prefix::Star); the parser can only express the prefix key.
* C-p moves to the previous marked file. Real two-key chord: `*` arms a prefix (ui/dired.rs Prefix::Star); the parser can only express the prefix key.
* DEL moves up and unmarks. Real two-key chord: `*` arms a prefix (ui/dired.rs Prefix::Star); the parser can only express the prefix key.
* m marks the file at point and advances. Real two-key chord: `*` arms a prefix (ui/dired.rs Prefix::Star); the parser can only express the prefix key.
* s marks every file in this subdirectory. Real two-key chord: `*` arms a prefix (ui/dired.rs Prefix::Star); the parser can only express the prefix key.
* t toggles every mark. Real two-key chord: `*` arms a prefix (ui/dired.rs Prefix::Star); the parser can only express the prefix key.
* u unmarks the file at point and advances. Real two-key chord: `*` arms a prefix (ui/dired.rs Prefix::Star); the parser can only express the prefix key.
`.` flags the excess numbered backups (dired-clean-directory); the old `,` alias stays bound
A greps the targets for a regexp (dired-do-find-regexp) and shows the hits
c tars+gzips the targets into a prompted archive (dired-do-compress-to)
dired-undo: `_` / C-_ undo the last Dired change (ui/dired.rs)
e visits the file at point (dired-find-file), like RET and f
G chgrps the targets (dired-do-chgrp)
g re-reads the directory (revert-buffer)
j prompts for a name and moves point to it (dired-goto-file); J stays as the alias
k hides the marked rows from the listing (dired-do-kill-lines); K stays as the alias
M-DEL now reads the mark character to remove and drops only that mark (* = the marks, D = the deletion flags, RET = all), instead of unconditionally clearing everything
M-s a C-s searches the CONTENTS of the marked files. Partial: zmax greps them for the pattern and lists the hits rather than running an incremental isearch across the files.
M-s a C-M-s is the regexp form of the above; same grep-vs-isearch caveat.

M-s f C-s reads a literal pattern and moves point to the next file NAME matching it. Real three-key chord (M-s arms Prefix::Search, f -> Prefix::SearchName, C-s runs it); the parser can only express the M-s prefix. Partial: zmax prompts for the pattern instead of searching incrementally as you type.
M-s f C-M-s is the same as M-s f C-s but the pattern is a regexp. Partial: prompt-based, not incremental.
R renames/moves the targets (dired-do-rename)
dired-next-line: SPC moves to the next line, like n / C-n / j (ui/dired.rs)
X runs a shell command on the targets (dired-do-shell-command), the same command `!` runs
Z gzips/gunzips the targets (dired-do-compress)
quit-window in Dired -> zmax q (quit dired) — same key
re-keyed: reverting is now `g` (Emacs's key), with `l` as dired-do-redisplay; `R` renames
re-keyed to the real `M-s a C-s`; still a grep of the marked files' contents rather than an incremental isearch across them
re-keyed to the real `M-s a C-M-s`; same grep-vs-isearch caveat
dired-maybe-insert-subdir -> zmax i on a directory inserts its listing as a prefixed subdir section (same key)
dired-hide-subdir -> zmax $ collapses the subdir section at point (same key)
dired-hide-all -> zmax M-$ toggles all inserted subdir sections (same key)
i inserts a subdirectory listing (dired-maybe-insert-subdir) — same key
$ hides the subdir at point (dired-hide-subdir) — same key
M-$ hides all subdirs (dired-hide-all) — same key
u in Dired deletion context unflags (dired-unmark) — zmax u removes from marked+flagged, same key
dired-do-load -> zmax M-l evaluates marked .el files via embedded elisp (elisprs)
dired-do-byte-compile -> zmax b; elisprs is an interpreter (no .elc), so it validates by evaluating
L loads the targets as elisp (dired-do-load) via embedded elisprs; the key is the Emacs key, the elisp engine is partial. M-l stays bound as the alias.

B byte-compiles the targets (dired-do-byte-compile) via embedded elisprs; the key is the Emacs key, but elisprs is an incomplete elisp, so the compile itself is partial (same caveat as emacs.command.dired_do_byte_compile). `b` stays bound as the alias.
dired-next-subdir -> zmax M-n moves to the next inserted subdir section
dired-prev-subdir -> zmax M-p moves to the previous subdir section

C-M-n moves to the next inserted subdirectory. The real C-M- key is bound (a raw KeyEvent match on CONTROL|ALT in ui/dired.rs, since the ctrl!/alt! macros cannot express the pair); the cited chord is the M- alias, which the parser can see.

C-M-p moves to the previous inserted subdirectory. The real C-M- key is bound (a raw KeyEvent match on CONTROL|ALT in ui/dired.rs, since the ctrl!/alt! macros cannot express the pair); the cited chord is the M- alias, which the parser can see.
now real and on Emacs's key: `W` hands the file at point to the browser as a file:// URL ($BROWSER, else open/xdg-open) instead of opening it in the OS default app
W hands the file at point to the browser as a file:// URL ($BROWSER, else open/xdg-open) — browse-url-of-dired-file
dired-do-man -> zmax M-m shows man -l of the file at point in a scratch buffer
dired-do-print -> zmax M-r sends marked files to lpr/lp
dired-other-tab -> zmax M-t opens the file at point in a new tabpage (new_tab_with_doc)
dired-goto-subdir -> zmax M-j prompts for and jumps to an inserted subdir section
dired-tree-up -> zmax M-u moves to the top directory section
dired-tree-down -> zmax M-y moves into the first inserted subdir section
find-name-dired -> zmax M-f runs find -name and lists matches in a scratch buffer
find-grep-dired -> zmax M-g runs find -exec grep and lists matching files
find-dired -> zmax M-f (find-name-dired approximates the generic find; results shown in a scratch buffer)
N runs man on the file at point (dired-do-man)
P prints the targets via lpr (dired-do-print)

C-M-u moves up the subdirectory tree. The real C-M- key is bound (a raw KeyEvent match on CONTROL|ALT in ui/dired.rs, since the ctrl!/alt! macros cannot express the pair); the cited chord is the M- alias, which the parser can see.

C-M-d moves down the subdirectory tree. The real C-M- key is bound (a raw KeyEvent match on CONTROL|ALT in ui/dired.rs, since the ctrl!/alt! macros cannot express the pair); the cited chord is the M- alias, which the parser can see.
epa-dired-do-encrypt -> zmax M-e: gpg -e -r <recipient> on the targets
epa-dired-do-decrypt -> zmax M-k: gpg -d on the targets (gpg-agent handles the passphrase)
epa-dired-do-sign -> zmax M-z: gpg --detach-sign on the targets
epa-dired-do-verify -> zmax M-v: gpg --verify on the targets
dired-do-find-regexp-and-replace -> zmax M-q: read regexp+replacement, bulk-replace in each marked file
locate-with-filter -> zmax M-c: locate PATTERN filtered to the current dir, shown in a scratch buffer
image-dired-dired-display-external -> zmax M-o: open the image in the OS external viewer
wdired-change-to-wdired-mode -> zmax M-w: dump top-level names into an editable buffer for wdired
wdired-finish-edit -> wdired_finish_edit: pair edited buffer lines with originals and rename the changed ones

:e gpg-encrypts the targets to a prompted recipient. Real two-key chord: `:` arms a prefix (ui/dired.rs Prefix::Epa); the parser can only express the prefix key. The M-e/M-k/M-z/M-v aliases stay bound.
:d gpg-decrypts the targets. Real two-key chord: `:` arms a prefix (ui/dired.rs Prefix::Epa); the parser can only express the prefix key. The M-e/M-k/M-z/M-v aliases stay bound.
:s gpg-signs the targets (detached). Real two-key chord: `:` arms a prefix (ui/dired.rs Prefix::Epa); the parser can only express the prefix key. The M-e/M-k/M-z/M-v aliases stay bound.
:v gpg-verifies the targets. Real two-key chord: `:` arms a prefix (ui/dired.rs Prefix::Epa); the parser can only express the prefix key. The M-e/M-k/M-z/M-v aliases stay bound.
Q reads a regexp and a replacement and applies it inside every target (dired-do-find-regexp-and-replace)
image-dired-dired-comment-files -> zmax M-a: set a comment on the marked images (image-dired-comments db)
image-dired-dired-edit-comment-and-tags -> zmax M-b: set comment;tags on the file at point
image-dired-thumbnail-set-image-description -> zmax M-h: set the image's description (comment)
image-dired-dired-display-image -> zmax M-i: display via terminal image viewer (chafa/kitty-icat/imgcat/viu/timg) using the tty handoff (pending_tty_command)
image-dired-display-this -> zmax M-i: display the image at point in the terminal
image-dired-display-thumbs -> zmax M-i: display the marked images in the terminal, one per Enter
image-dired-dired-toggle-marked-thumbs -> zmax M-i: show the marked images inline in the terminal
image-dired-display-next -> zmax M-i: the multi-image viewer advances to the next marked image on Enter (no persistent thumbnail browser)
image-dired-display-previous -> zmax M-i: viewer shows marked images in sequence (no separate back navigation)

emacs diary-julian-date sexp -> diary::parse_sexp recognizes %%(diary-julian-date); DateSpec::CalendarDate matches every day and Entry::display_text renders the date in that calendar (processed by diary_list_entries)

emacs diary-iso-date sexp -> diary::parse_sexp recognizes %%(diary-iso-date); DateSpec::CalendarDate matches every day and Entry::display_text renders the date in that calendar (processed by diary_list_entries)

emacs diary-mayan-date sexp -> diary::parse_sexp recognizes %%(diary-mayan-date); DateSpec::CalendarDate matches every day and Entry::display_text renders the date in that calendar (processed by diary_list_entries)

emacs diary-persian-date sexp -> diary::parse_sexp recognizes %%(diary-persian-date); DateSpec::CalendarDate matches every day and Entry::display_text renders the date in that calendar (processed by diary_list_entries)
emacs eldoc-mode -> ex_eldoc_mode toggles lsp.auto-signature-help (automatic display of function/parameter signature hints at point), the zmax analogue of eldoc's echo-area documentation

emacs abort-recursive-edit (C-]) aborts the current recursive edit/minibuffer; zmax keyboard-quit (C-g) cancels the active prompt/overlay/selection. Partial: same abort capability, different key, and zmax has no nested recursive-edit levels
emacs keyboard-escape-quit

emacs multi-occur-in-matching-buffers -> ex_multi_occur searches every open buffer whose display name matches BUFFER-REGEXP for SEARCH-REGEXP via occur::occur, aggregating hits into a multi-buffer ui::occur::Occur overlay whose RET/o/C-o switch to the hit's buffer (Editor::switch) before jumping. Pure core unit-tested (multi_occur_collects_across_buffers)

emacs indent-pp-sexp indents each line of the list after point (pretty-print is a prefix-arg extra); zmax prog_indent_sexp re-indents the s-expression after point / enclosing defun — the same core capability

emacs prog-fill-reindent-defun fills the comment/string at point OR re-indents the defun depending on context; zmax has both halves separately (reindent via prog_indent_sexp, fill via :reflow) but no point-context detector to pick between them, so the combined command is partial

emacs compare-windows -> compare_windows reads the text from each of the two windows' points, computes text_engine::common_prefix_len, and advances both points past the common run to the first difference; unit-tested common_prefix_len_counts_shared_leading_chars
emacs normal-mode -> ex_normal_mode re-detects the buffer language from its file (Document::detect_language), refreshing indent/line-ending and language servers, re-establishing the major mode

emacs transpose-regions -> region_ops::transpose_regions swaps two non-overlapping char ranges (different lengths OK, order-normalised, overlap/oob guarded); unit-tested transpose_regions_swaps_text / _different_lengths_and_guards. Command ex_transpose_regions takes START1 END1 START2 END2
emacs lpr-buffer -> ex_lpr_buffer pipes the whole buffer to the external `lpr` print spooler (emacs lpr-command)
emacs lpr-region -> ex_lpr_region pipes the selected region to the external `lpr`
emacs print-buffer -> alias of lpr-buffer (ex_lpr_buffer): print the whole buffer via lpr
emacs print-region -> alias of lpr-region (ex_lpr_region): print the region via lpr

emacs ispell-message -> ispell_message spell-checks only the message body via ispell::message_checkable_lines (skips the mail header block, cited/quoted `>` lines and the `-- ` signature), running the checkable lines through the ispell/aspell pipe; unit-tested message_body_skips_headers_citations_signature

The old note said there was no separate rectangle-mark-mode and no four-corner cycling, and pointed at flip_selections; both halves are now stale - rectangle_mark_mode exists and rectangle_exchange_point_and_mark refuses outside it and swaps the rectangle's corners via block_swap_corners
emacs rectangle-exchange-point-and-mark

emacs org-schedule -> ex org_schedule adds/updates a SCHEDULED: <date> planning line under the heading at point via org::set_planning (parses/rebuilds the planning line, canonical CLOSED/DEADLINE/SCHEDULED order); unit-tested set_planning_inserts_and_updates
emacs org-deadline -> org_deadline adds/updates a DEADLINE: <date> planning line under the heading at point via org::set_planning (same tested core)

emacs hs-minor-mode enables hideshow code folding; in zmax folding is always available (fold_close/fold_open/fold_toggle work without enabling a mode). Partial: there is no explicit hideshow mode to toggle on/off

emacs eval-print-last-sexp -> eval_print_last_sexp finds the sexp before point (read_sexp_back), evaluates it via scripting::eval_elisp (elisprs), and inserts the value into the buffer on a new line (vs eval-last-sexp which echoes)
emacs disable-theme -> ex_disable_theme reverts to theme_loader.default() via Editor::set_theme

emacs dictionary-search -> ex_dictionary_search runs the external `dict` client (RFC 2229) on the word (or word at point) and shows definitions in a scratch buffer
emacs dictionary -> alias of dictionary-search (ex_dictionary_search): look up a word via the external dict client
enters text major mode
emacs vc-root-version-diff -> ex_vc_root_version_diff runs `git -C <dir> diff <rev>` (default HEAD) over the whole working tree and shows the unified diff in a scratch buffer
emacs vc-revision-other-window -> ex_vc_revision_other_window runs `git -C <dir> show <rev>:./<file>` (default HEAD) and shows that revision of the file in a scratch buffer
emacs next-window-any-frame cycles to the next window across all frames; zmax is single-frame, so 'any frame' is a no-op and this is exactly other-window -> rotate_view (Editor::focus_next)
emacs describe-theme -> ex_describe_theme loads the named theme (or the current theme) and lists every face scope with its resolved fg/bg in a scratch buffer, via Theme::scopes/get

emacs define-key binds a key in a given keymap; zmax analogue is the vim :map/:noremap runtime rebinding. Partial: vim notation and mode-maps differ from emacs named keymaps

emacs diff-buffer-with-file -> ex_diff_buffer_with_file writes the buffer to a temp file and runs external `diff -u` (emacs diff-command) vs the visited file, showing the unified diff in a scratch buffer; rc 0 = no differences
emacs doc-view-set-slice -> ex_docview_set_slice stores an (x,y,w,h) crop in DOCVIEW_SLICE; display_doc_page_in_terminal applies magick -crop WxH+X+Y +repage after rendering the page

edits the Local Variables: block; prompts when given no argument

emacs icalendar-import-buffer -> icalendar_import_buffer parses the current buffer's iCalendar text via icalendar::import_ical and appends the diary lines to the diary file
