* ? reads a mark character and removes that mark everywhere (RET = all). Real two-key chord: `*` arms a prefix (ui/dired.rs Prefix::Star); the parser can only express the prefix key.
* N echoes the count and total size of the marked files. Real two-key chord: `*` arms a prefix (ui/dired.rs Prefix::Star); the parser can only express the prefix key.
E opens the targets with the OS opener (dired-do-open)

I runs info on the file at point (dired-do-info): zmax has no Info reader, so the Info file's text is shown in a scratch buffer with the node separators rendered as rules — the content, without node-to-node navigation.
a lists the month's holidays in the output pane (calendar-list-holidays); the pane is the window Emacs pops up, scrolled with SPC/DEL
m marks every date of the displayed month that has a diary entry (diary-mark-entries)
SPC scrolls the output pane (scroll-other-window) — the pane holding the holiday/diary/conversion listings; DEL and S-SPC scroll it back
M-= reports the inclusive day count of the region (calendar-count-days-region); C-SPC / C-@ set the mark
C-c C-l redraws the calendar, re-reading the diary file (calendar-redraw). Real two-key chord: C-c arms Prefix::CtrlC
C-x [ moves back one year (calendar-backward-year). Real two-key chord: C-x arms Prefix::CtrlX (which also carries C-x ], C-x <, C-x > and C-x C-x)
C-x ] moves forward one year (calendar-forward-year)
g d prompts for a date and moves point there (calendar-goto-date). Real two-key chord: `g` arms Prefix::Goto
g D prompts for a day-of-year (and optional year) and moves point there (calendar-goto-day-of-year)
g w prompts for an ISO year/week/weekday and moves point there (calendar-iso-goto-week)
g m is the Mayan sub-map: g m l goes to a long-count date, g m n h/t/c and g m p h/t/c to the next/previous haab, tzolkin and calendar-round date

t d / t m / t y write the LaTeX source of a printable day/month/year calendar to ~/calendar-*.tex (cal-tex). Partial: zmax writes the .tex and reports the path; Emacs then runs LaTeX on it, which zmax does not do.
H m / H y write a browsable HTML calendar of the month / year (holidays marked) to ~/calendar-*.html (cal-html). Real two-key chord: H arms Prefix::Html

i d appends a one-off entry for the date at point to the diary file (~/diary) and re-reads it so the grid marks it at once. Real two-key chord: `i` arms Prefix::Insert; the parser can only express the prefix key. The written line is unit-tested to parse back into the DateSpec it means (ui/calendar.rs, inserted_diary_lines_parse_back_to_the_right_spec).

i w appends a weekly entry (this weekday, by name) to the diary file (~/diary) and re-reads it so the grid marks it at once. Real two-key chord: `i` arms Prefix::Insert; the parser can only express the prefix key. The written line is unit-tested to parse back into the DateSpec it means (ui/calendar.rs, inserted_diary_lines_parse_back_to_the_right_spec).

i m appends a monthly entry (this day of every month, as the wildcard-month sexp form) to the diary file (~/diary) and re-reads it so the grid marks it at once. Real two-key chord: `i` arms Prefix::Insert; the parser can only express the prefix key. The written line is unit-tested to parse back into the DateSpec it means (ui/calendar.rs, inserted_diary_lines_parse_back_to_the_right_spec).

i y appends a yearly entry (this month/day) to the diary file (~/diary) and re-reads it so the grid marks it at once. Real two-key chord: `i` arms Prefix::Insert; the parser can only express the prefix key. The written line is unit-tested to parse back into the DateSpec it means (ui/calendar.rs, inserted_diary_lines_parse_back_to_the_right_spec).

i a appends an anniversary entry (%%(diary-anniversary ...)) to the diary file (~/diary) and re-reads it so the grid marks it at once. Real two-key chord: `i` arms Prefix::Insert; the parser can only express the prefix key. The written line is unit-tested to parse back into the DateSpec it means (ui/calendar.rs, inserted_diary_lines_parse_back_to_the_right_spec).

i b appends a block entry over the region, mark -> point (%%(diary-block ...)); with no mark it refuses to the diary file (~/diary) and re-reads it so the grid marks it at once. Real two-key chord: `i` arms Prefix::Insert; the parser can only express the prefix key. The written line is unit-tested to parse back into the DateSpec it means (ui/calendar.rs, inserted_diary_lines_parse_back_to_the_right_spec).

i c appends a cyclic entry, reading `N TEXT` (%%(diary-cyclic ...)) to the diary file (~/diary) and re-reads it so the grid marks it at once. Real two-key chord: `i` arms Prefix::Insert; the parser can only express the prefix key. The written line is unit-tested to parse back into the DateSpec it means (ui/calendar.rs, inserted_diary_lines_parse_back_to_the_right_spec).

g c/j/h/i/p/k/e/f/b/a prompt for a date on the ISO / Julian / Hebrew / Islamic / Persian / Coptic / Ethiopic / French Revolutionary / Baha'i / astronomical calendar and move point to the Gregorian date it names (each via the matching fixed_from_* in zmax_core::calendar; round-trips are unit-tested). Partial: `g C` (calendar-chinese-goto-date) is absent — zmax_core::calendar has no Chinese calendar.
p d prints the day-of-year of the date at point (calendar-print-day-of-year), now under the real `p` prefix rather than on a bare `p`.
C-c ' sets the drawing direction to NE. Real two-key chord: C-c arms a prefix in ui/picture.rs (C-c C-c exits picture mode, as in Emacs); the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

C-c . sets the drawing direction to south. Real two-key chord: C-c arms a prefix in ui/picture.rs (C-c C-c exits picture mode, as in Emacs); the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.
C-c / sets the drawing direction to SW. Real two-key chord: C-c arms a prefix in ui/picture.rs (C-c C-c exits picture mode, as in Emacs); the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

C-c < sets the drawing direction to west. Real two-key chord: C-c arms a prefix in ui/picture.rs (C-c C-c exits picture mode, as in Emacs); the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

C-c > sets the drawing direction to east. Real two-key chord: C-c arms a prefix in ui/picture.rs (C-c C-c exits picture mode, as in Emacs); the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.
C-c \ sets the drawing direction to SE. Real two-key chord: C-c arms a prefix in ui/picture.rs (C-c C-c exits picture mode, as in Emacs); the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

C-c ^ sets the drawing direction to north. Real two-key chord: C-c arms a prefix in ui/picture.rs (C-c C-c exits picture mode, as in Emacs); the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.
C-c ` sets the drawing direction to NW. Real two-key chord: C-c arms a prefix in ui/picture.rs (C-c C-c exits picture mode, as in Emacs); the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

C-c UP sets the drawing direction to north. Real two-key chord: C-c arms a prefix in ui/picture.rs (C-c C-c exits picture mode, as in Emacs); the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

C-c DOWN sets the drawing direction to south. Real two-key chord: C-c arms a prefix in ui/picture.rs (C-c C-c exits picture mode, as in Emacs); the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

C-c LEFT sets the drawing direction to west. Real two-key chord: C-c arms a prefix in ui/picture.rs (C-c C-c exits picture mode, as in Emacs); the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

C-c RIGHT sets the drawing direction to east. Real two-key chord: C-c arms a prefix in ui/picture.rs (C-c C-c exits picture mode, as in Emacs); the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

C-c Home sets the drawing direction to NW. Real two-key chord: C-c arms a prefix in ui/picture.rs (C-c C-c exits picture mode, as in Emacs); the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

C-c End sets the drawing direction to SW. Real two-key chord: C-c arms a prefix in ui/picture.rs (C-c C-c exits picture mode, as in Emacs); the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

C-c prior sets the drawing direction to NE. Real two-key chord: C-c arms a prefix in ui/picture.rs (C-c C-c exits picture mode, as in Emacs); the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

C-c PageUp sets the drawing direction to NE. Real two-key chord: C-c arms a prefix in ui/picture.rs (C-c C-c exits picture mode, as in Emacs); the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

C-c next sets the drawing direction to SE. Real two-key chord: C-c arms a prefix in ui/picture.rs (C-c C-c exits picture mode, as in Emacs); the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

C-c PageDown sets the drawing direction to SE. Real two-key chord: C-c arms a prefix in ui/picture.rs (C-c C-c exits picture mode, as in Emacs); the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

C-c C-f steps point ALONG the drawing direction (picture-motion). Real two-key chord: C-c arms a prefix in ui/picture.rs (C-c C-c exits picture mode, as in Emacs); the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

C-c C-b steps point against the drawing direction (picture-motion-reverse). Real two-key chord: C-c arms a prefix in ui/picture.rs (C-c C-c exits picture mode, as in Emacs); the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

C-c C-d deletes the character under point and slides the rest of the row left (picture-delete-char). Real two-key chord: C-c arms a prefix in ui/picture.rs (C-c C-c exits picture mode, as in Emacs); the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

C-c C-k clears the mark->point rectangle and SAVES it for yanking (picture-clear-rectangle). Real two-key chord: C-c arms a prefix in ui/picture.rs (C-c C-c exits picture mode, as in Emacs); the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

C-c C-y overlays the saved rectangle at point, overwriting what it covers (picture-yank-rectangle). Real two-key chord: C-c arms a prefix in ui/picture.rs (C-c C-c exits picture mode, as in Emacs); the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

C-c C-w r clears the rectangle into register `r` (picture-clear-rectangle-to-register). Real two-key chord: C-c arms a prefix in ui/picture.rs (C-c C-c exits picture mode, as in Emacs); the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

C-c C-x r overlays the rectangle saved in register `r` (picture-yank-rectangle-from-register). Real two-key chord: C-c arms a prefix in ui/picture.rs (C-c C-c exits picture mode, as in Emacs); the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

C-c TAB sets the tab stops from the words on the current row (picture-set-tab-stops). Real two-key chord: C-c arms a prefix in ui/picture.rs (C-c C-c exits picture mode, as in Emacs); the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

M-TAB (and C-M-i, matched as a raw CONTROL|ALT KeyEvent) moves point to the column beneath the next interesting character in the row above — a non-blank preceded by whitespace — or to column 0 when there is none (picture-tab-search).

C-c C-a moves to the process mark / beginning of line (comint-bol-or-process-mark). Real two-key chord: C-c arms the comint job-control prefix in ui/comint.rs; the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

C-c C-b moves point back over one `;`/`|`/`&`-separated shell command (shell-backward-command). Real two-key chord: C-c arms the comint job-control prefix in ui/comint.rs; the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.
C-c C-c sends SIGINT to the child (comint-interrupt-subjob). Real two-key chord: C-c arms the comint job-control prefix in ui/comint.rs; the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

C-c C-e scrolls to the end of the output (comint-show-maximum-output). Real two-key chord: C-c arms the comint job-control prefix in ui/comint.rs; the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

C-c C-f moves point forward over one shell command (shell-forward-command). Real two-key chord: C-c arms the comint job-control prefix in ui/comint.rs; the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

C-c C-l lists the input history in the buffer (comint-dynamic-list-input-ring). Real two-key chord: C-c arms the comint job-control prefix in ui/comint.rs; the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.
C-c C-n moves to the next prompt (comint-next-prompt). Real two-key chord: C-c arms the comint job-control prefix in ui/comint.rs; the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

C-c C-o deletes the last command's output (comint-delete-output). Real two-key chord: C-c arms the comint job-control prefix in ui/comint.rs; the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

C-c C-p moves to the previous prompt (comint-previous-prompt). Real two-key chord: C-c arms the comint job-control prefix in ui/comint.rs; the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

C-c C-r scrolls the last output to the top (comint-show-output). Real two-key chord: C-c arms the comint job-control prefix in ui/comint.rs; the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

C-c C-s writes the last command's output to a prompted file (comint-write-output). Real two-key chord: C-c arms the comint job-control prefix in ui/comint.rs; the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.
C-c C-u kills the input line (comint-kill-input). Real two-key chord: C-c arms the comint job-control prefix in ui/comint.rs; the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

C-c C-w kills the word before point on the input line (backward-kill-word). Real two-key chord: C-c arms the comint job-control prefix in ui/comint.rs; the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

C-c C-x inserts the input that followed the one last recalled (comint-get-next-from-history). Real two-key chord: C-c arms the comint job-control prefix in ui/comint.rs; the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.
C-c C-z sends SIGTSTP to the child (comint-stop-subjob). Real two-key chord: C-c arms the comint job-control prefix in ui/comint.rs; the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.
C-c C-\ sends SIGQUIT to the child (comint-quit-subjob). Real two-key chord: C-c arms the comint job-control prefix in ui/comint.rs; the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

C-c RET copies the old input at point onto the input line (comint-copy-old-input). Real two-key chord: C-c arms the comint job-control prefix in ui/comint.rs; the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

C-c . inserts the last argument of the previous input (comint-insert-previous-argument, `!$`). Real two-key chord: C-c arms the comint job-control prefix in ui/comint.rs; the report parser can only express the prefix key as a chord.

TAB completes the file name before point (completion-at-point): a unique candidate is inserted whole, several insert their longest common prefix, and TAB with no progress left lists the candidates in the buffer.
M-? lists, in the buffer, the file names that complete the one before point (comint-dynamic-list-filename-completions).

M-r reads a pattern and yanks the newest older input containing it onto the input line (comint-history-isearch-backward-regexp). Partial: zmax prompts for the pattern and matches it as a substring, rather than searching the history incrementally as a regexp.

C-M-l scrolls the last output to the top (comint-show-output), the same command C-c C-r runs. The real C-M-l key is bound (a raw CONTROL|ALT KeyEvent match in ui/comint.rs, which the ctrl!/alt! macros cannot express), so the cited chord is the C-c prefix that also reaches it.
b buries (closes) the reader — rmail-bury; the old `z` alias stays bound
b in the summary pane buries it back to the message view (rmail-summary-bury)

c returns to the outgoing message previously being composed (rmail-continue). Partial: zmax has no named draft buffer, so it switches to the newest unsaved buffer whose first line is a `To:` header — the compose template — and reports when there is none.
C-o appends the message, as displayed, to a prompted file (rmail-output-as-seen); `O` stays bound as the alias
S-SPC scrolls the message body back, like DEL (rmail binds both)

M-m retries a failed message (rmail-retry-failure): it finds the copy of the original after the unsent-message separator in the bounce, and opens a draft addressed to the ORIGINAL recipient with its subject and body — not to the mailer daemon. Refuses on a message that carries no such copy.

C-M-f summarizes by sender (rmail-summary-by-senders). The real C-M- key is bound (a raw CONTROL|ALT KeyEvent match in ui/rmail.rs, which the ctrl!/alt! macros cannot express); the cited chord is the single-key alias, which the parser can see.

C-M-r summarizes by recipient (rmail-summary-by-recipients). The real C-M- key is bound (a raw CONTROL|ALT KeyEvent match in ui/rmail.rs, which the ctrl!/alt! macros cannot express); the cited chord is the single-key alias, which the parser can see.

C-M-t summarizes by subject/topic (rmail-summary-by-topic). The real C-M- key is bound (a raw CONTROL|ALT KeyEvent match in ui/rmail.rs, which the ctrl!/alt! macros cannot express); the cited chord is the single-key alias, which the parser can see.

C-M-s summarizes by regexp (rmail-summary-by-regexp). The real C-M- key is bound (a raw CONTROL|ALT KeyEvent match in ui/rmail.rs, which the ctrl!/alt! macros cannot express); the cited chord is the single-key alias, which the parser can see.

C-M-l summarizes by label (rmail-summary-by-labels). The real C-M- key is bound (a raw CONTROL|ALT KeyEvent match in ui/rmail.rs, which the ctrl!/alt! macros cannot express); the cited chord is the single-key alias, which the parser can see.

C-M-n moves to the next message carrying a prompted label. The real C-M- key is bound (a raw CONTROL|ALT KeyEvent match in ui/rmail.rs, which the ctrl!/alt! macros cannot express); the cited chord is the single-key alias, which the parser can see.

C-M-p moves to the previous message carrying a prompted label. The real C-M- key is bound (a raw CONTROL|ALT KeyEvent match in ui/rmail.rs, which the ctrl!/alt! macros cannot express); the cited chord is the single-key alias, which the parser can see.

S sorts the rows by the column the column cursor names (tabulated-list-sort) — Size numerically, the rest lexically — and a second S on the same column reverses it. Partial: the column cursor is moved with M-left/M-right (Emacs puts point in the header cell, which a list overlay has no notion of).

{ narrows the column the column cursor names by one character (tabulated-list-narrow-current-column); the rows re-render clipped to the new width. Partial: the column is selected with M-left/M-right rather than by where point sits in the row.
} widens that column by one character (tabulated-list-widen-current-column). Same column-cursor caveat as `{`.

isearch-repeat-forward: next match; empty string recalls the last search — active under the emacs/helix presets; under the shipped spacemacs/vim default this chord keeps vim's command-line meaning
isearch-repeat-backward: previous match, absolute — active under the emacs/helix presets; under the shipped spacemacs/vim default this chord keeps vim's command-line meaning

isearch-yank-word-or-char: appends the buffer word at the match end and re-searches — active under the emacs/helix presets; under the shipped spacemacs/vim default this chord keeps vim's command-line meaning
isearch-yank-kill: appends the top of the kill ring — active under the emacs/helix presets; under the shipped spacemacs/vim default this chord keeps vim's command-line meaning
isearch-quote-char: the next character enters the string quoted — active under the emacs/helix presets; under the shipped spacemacs/vim default this chord keeps vim's command-line meaning
isearch-abort: quits, restoring the start position — active under the emacs/helix presets; under the shipped spacemacs/vim default this chord keeps vim's command-line meaning
isearch-delete-char: drops the last char and the search backs up
isearch-exit: stops on the shown match, pushes the jump, stores the pattern
isearch-toggle-case-fold, applied as an inline (?i) flag on the pattern
isearch-toggle-regexp: regexp <-> literal (the string is quoted)

isearch-occur: ends the search and opens occur on the pattern
isearch-yank-line: appends the rest of the line and re-searches

the same menu tree flattened into a keyboard-driven text list

View-scroll-page-backward

a later setq in any document creates a local binding there instead of changing the global

reads the global value, ignoring the local binding

xdb in a comint buffer (GUD)
sdb in a comint buffer (GUD)

sends the top-level form at point to the inferior Lisp; the form is found by a parser that skips parens inside strings, comments and ?( char literals
re-reads ~/.mailrc (alias/group lines, backslash continuations)
merges a second alias file, its definitions winning
defines an alias AND appends it to ~/.mailrc, so it survives a restart
picker; inserts the recursively expanded address list (cycle-safe)
expands the alias word before point
in a draft's header block it completes the mail alias; elsewhere an ordinary indent, as message-tab is
jumps to Fcc:, CREATING the header when the draft has none
jumps to Reply-To:, creating it if absent
jumps to Followup-To:, creating it if absent
inserts the message being replied to, cited: attribution from its From:/Date:, body behind message-yank-prefix
sets the citation prefix message-yank-original reads
refills the cited paragraphs, keeping each quote level's prefix
writes into the tree's .dir-locals.el (a real alist reader/writer)
removes the variable, dropping the entry when it empties
reads the file's prop line and Local Variables block and sets each for the whole tree
resolves the dir-locals in force (every .dir-locals.el from the root down, plus registered classes, deepest winning) and writes them into the file
same resolution, into the first line's -*- prop line
registers a named set of directory-local variables
binds a registered class to a directory; the class's variables really do appear in the lookup
pops every modal overlay back to the editor, where keyboard-quit only cancels the innermost
opens a draft pre-filled with the version, OS/arch and tracker URL
highlights what an alignment rule matches, using the hi-lock overlay the renderer already draws
removes the align-rule highlighting
the regexp really is highlighted over the syntax highlighting, but it is not scoped to a major mode and the face is by index rather than named
removes the regexp; same mode-scoping caveat
edits the tab-stop column list picture-tab actually uses, so an edit is immediately observable
emacs narrows the window to column 72; a TUI cannot, so zmax marks column 72 with a ruler — the same warning, a different mechanism
the column-72 ruler, removed on the next key
re-encodes the region with the coding it was mistakenly read as, then decodes those bytes with the right one (the mojibake round trip)
enters outline major mode; its C-c C-* keymap comes alive (Document::major_mode)
outline-hide-by-heading-regexp: prompts for the regexp and folds every heading whose line matches
outline-show-by-heading-regexp: prompts for the regexp and unfolds every heading whose line matches
org-schedule: prompts for a date and writes the SCHEDULED: line under the heading
org-deadline: prompts for a date and writes the DEADLINE: line under the heading
View-exit: view_mode toggles, so on a view buffer `e` leaves view-mode
View-quit -> view_mode: leaves view-mode, but does not bury the buffer
eval-print-last-sexp on C-j: zmax's lisp-interaction-mode is an elisp scratch buffer and the overlay key is the language, so the chord is live in every elisp buffer (Normal only)
C-M-x: lisp-eval-defun sends the top-level form at point to the inferior Lisp (run-lisp)
C-M-x: lisp-eval-defun sends the top-level form at point to the inferior Lisp (run-lisp)
message-yank-original: cites the message being replied to behind message-yank-prefix
message-fill-yanked-message -> mail_fill_yanked_message: refills the cited paragraphs, keeping each quote level
mml-attach-file: inserts the MML attachment tag for a chosen file
M-a in the C-like modes: c-beginning-of-statement
M-e in the C-like modes: c-end-of-statement
gud-up: select the stack frame one level up
gud-down: select the stack frame one level down
gud-print: print the expression at point
gud-stepi: step one machine instruction
gud-refresh: redraw and scroll the debugger output to the newest line
gud-jump: set the debugger's execution point to the current source line
gud-watch: watch the expression at point
menu-bar-open on F10 in the emacs keymap; the vim base keeps F10 on the debugger's step-over
tmm-menubar: the text menu bar, the menu tree flattened into one keyboard-driven list
M-X (execute-extended-command-for-buffer) -> command_palette: the same palette, not filtered to the buffer's commands
M-S-x is M-X (execute-extended-command-for-buffer) -> command_palette: not filtered to the buffer's commands
ESC ESC ESC: keyboard-escape-quit, which is what aborts an isearch
insert-buffer: insert another buffer's text at point
rename-buffer: prompts for the new buffer name
C-x #: server-edit - release the waiting client (was the command_palette fallback; there is an emacs server now)
command_palette fallback: comment-set-column has no port
command_palette fallback: kmacro-query has no port
vc-edit-next-command -> git_status: no port that edits the next VC command before it runs
buffer_picker
universal-coding-system-argument: the next command reads/writes in the chosen coding system (was the command_palette fallback)
set-file-name-coding-system (was the command_palette fallback)
set-keyboard-coding-system (was the command_palette fallback)
set-buffer-process-coding-system (was the command_palette fallback)
set-terminal-coding-system (was the command_palette fallback)
set-selection-coding-system (was the command_palette fallback)
set-next-selection-coding-system (was the command_palette fallback)
describe-input-method -> unicode_picker: zmax has no input methods; the picker inserts any character by name
tab-move -> :tabmove: moves the tab, but to the last position (emacs moves it one to the right)
C-x t t: other-tab-prefix - redirect the NEXT command's buffer display into a new tab (was goto_next_tabpage, which is C-x t o)
display-battery-mode: battery charge in the mode line
display-time: time of day in the mode line
describe a package -> package_search: the picker over the language-support packages, same as C-h P
ediff-patch-file -> diff_ediff_patch: applies the patch in the current buffer instead of prompting for one
go to the previous frame -> rotate_view_reverse: zmax has no frames, so it cycles windows
]t: go to the next frame -> other_frame (wraps, like emacs's). zmax has real frames now; it used to rotate windows
cycle through previously visited buffers -> goto_last_accessed_file: toggles to the last-accessed file, no ring

opens the face picker (list-faces-display); Enter puts the chosen face on the region as a face text property (Document::text_props). The attribute faces (bold/italic/underline/bold-italic/default) become attribute toggles; the rest keep their emacs name and are painted from the theme scope zmax_core::facemenu::theme_scope resolves them to
opens list-colors-display; the chosen color becomes an RGB foreground on the region's face text property, rendered by EditorView::doc_text_prop_highlights
as facemenu-set-foreground, but the background attribute
add-face-text-property with the bold attribute over every non-empty selection; merges with any face already there (bold over red = bold red)
as facemenu-set-bold, italic attribute
as facemenu-set-bold, bold+italic
as facemenu-set-bold, underline attribute (rendered with UnderlineStyle::Line)
puts the default face on the region, i.e. removes every face attribute from it - TextProps::add_face routes an empty delta to remove_face

clears only the face property; text hidden by an `invisible` property (hide-ifdef-mode, sgml-tags-invisible) stays hidden. Genuinely distinct from facemenu-remove-all, and unit-tested as such in text_props.rs
drops every text property from the region - faces and `invisible` alike, so hidden text reappears

decodes any text/enriched markup already in the buffer into face text properties (real transaction), makes the facemenu setters meaningful, and arms Document::save to re-encode the buffer as text/enriched so the faces reach disk. Divergence: RFC 1896 soft-newline folding is not implemented (that is emacs use-hard-newlines, which zmax lacks); newlines pass through verbatim

runs the buffer through its format converter; the only format zmax converts is text/enriched (zmax_core::enriched::decode) - strips the Content-Type header and the annotations and installs the faces they described as text properties. Errors when the buffer has no format annotations

hides the body of every preprocessor branch that is provably not compiled (#if 0, the #else of #if 1, an #ifdef of a macro the file never defines) with an `invisible` text property; running it again reveals them. Divergence: emacs evaluates conditions against hide-ifdef-env (populated by hide-ifdef-define, which zmax does not have) and hides every branch that is not true, so with the default empty env it hides every #ifdef body. zmax evaluates against the file's own #defines, positionally (so include guards are not hidden), and leaves an undecidable condition visible

shades the body of every provably-dead preprocessor branch with the `shadow` face as a face text property, so compiled-out code is visibly dimmed and stays dimmed while you edit; running it again clears it. Divergence: emacs prompts for a face per distinct condition (its cpp-edit buffer); this uses one face and offers no per-condition prompt

highlights the C constructs cwarn.el flags: an assignment in an if/while condition (if (a = b)) and a semicolon that silently empties the body (if (x);). Scans the visible lines each frame, so it tracks edits. Divergence: cwarn.el's third check - a & in a C++ function call, warning of pass-by-reference - is not ported; it needs the callee's declaration, which a line scan does not have
turns cwarn-mode on in every buffer (same checks, same divergence)

hides every markup tag with an `invisible` text property, leaving only the marked-up text; running it again brings them back. Tag ranges come from zmax_core::sgml::parse_tags, so comments/doctypes/PIs are handled. The buffer text is untouched - the file on disk still has its markup

buttonizes the URLs and e-mail addresses in the visible lines (zmax_core::goto_address::addresses - schemes, bare www., bare e-mails, with trailing-punctuation and unmatched-bracket trimming). :open-url (emacs goto-address-at-point) follows the one under the cursor

toggles whether the region is shaded. With it off the region still exists and every region command still acts on it, and the cursor is still drawn - it simply stops being highlighted, which is the pre-23 emacs behaviour. Read by EditorView::doc_selection_highlights

draws -> as an arrow, <= as a relation, lambda as a lambda, via grapheme overlays (the mechanism conceallevel uses), so the buffer text is byte-identical and point still walks every character - the same guarantee emacs' display property gives. Symbol table is per-language (zmax_core::prettify::symbols_for); a language with no table is a no-op, as in emacs. Recomputed on every edit in Document::apply_impl

draws the characters a terminal renders as nothing - C0 controls (as Unicode Control Pictures), DEL, C1 controls, zero-width spaces, bidi overrides - as a visible glyph. TAB/newline are left to the existing whitespace renderer
Command-line-mode mappings: a real table the prompt reads for every typed character. Not the keymap trie (which holds Normal/Insert/Select keys only)
same table as :cmap - a character translation cannot recurse, so noremap and map coincide
removes one Command-line-mode mapping; E31 when there is none
clears the Command-line-mode mapping table
Lang-Arg mappings - the character table 'keymap' loads and 'iminsert'/'imsearch' switch on; applied by insert-mode self-insert and the search prompt
same table as :lmap
removes one Lang-Arg mapping
clears the Lang-Arg table
introduces the {from} {to} pairs of a keymap file; :set keymap={name} reads the file and installs them. Typed at the command line it is vim's E105, as in vim
Terminal-mode mappings, read by the terminal panel before a key reaches the PTY
same table as :tmap
removes one Terminal-mode mapping
clears the Terminal-mode mapping table
E25: GUI cannot be used: Not enabled at compile time - the answer a terminal vim without GUI support gives, and zmax is a TUI
alias of :gui - E25
reports the editor area with no arguments, and with two resizes it (setting 'columns'/'lines'); a TUI cannot resize the terminal window itself
E188: Obtaining the window position is not implemented for this platform - a terminal vim's own answer
zmax's floating windows are the compositor's overlay layers (pickers, popups, panels); :fclose closes the topmost one
re-executes the editor binary with the same arguments (exec, so the terminal is handed straight over); a modified buffer blocks it with E37 unless ! is given
writes the hunk under the selection into the diff base - the other side of :diffthis, and what :diffget takes text from - so the difference is gone, as in vim
:filter[!] {pat} {cmd} runs the command and shows only the output lines matching the pattern; applied where every : listing goes (show_text_in_scratch) and consumed by the same call
replaces every occurrence of the word the last z= replaced, with the same replacement; E752 when there was no z=

`.http`/`.rest` request files: tree-sitter-http grammar (rest-nvim/tree-sitter-http @ db8b4398) with JSON/XML/GraphQL body injection, plus the kulala-ls language server. Editing and diagnostics only - the send-request-under-cursor command restclient.el binds to C-c C-c does not exist yet

zmax formats through a generic external-formatter subsystem: set formatter = { command = "prettier" } on any language in languages.toml and :format runs it. The layer's SPC m = = maps to format_selections. No prettier default is wired, because it would override LSP formatting for users without prettier installed
the same formatter slot as the prettier layer - js-beautify is another formatter = { command = ... } entry in languages.toml, run by format_selections / :format. No js-beautify default is wired

the file explorer with preview plus a full dired mode cover the ranger/deer browsing surface (navigate, enter, open, mark, rename, delete). The multi-column miller-view preview stack (z-/z+ column count, C-j/C-k preview scroll) is not implemented

the behaviors this layer rebinds already exist as commands: C-a beginning-of-line-or-code -> goto_first_nonwhitespace, C-w backward-kill-word-or-region -> delete_word_backward, M-q fill/unfill -> fill_region_as_paragraph. Auto-indent-on-paste (C-y with a prefix arg) is not implemented

dtrt-indent's whole capability - guess tabs-vs-spaces and width from the buffer's existing content - is engine behavior in zmax: auto_detect_indent_style (zmax-core/src/indent.rs:79) runs from Document::detect_indent_and_line_ending on every open and reload. The layer has no commands of its own; the cited tokens are the manual override, because the detector itself is not a command

Deft's core loop - filter-as-you-type over a directory of plain-text notes, by filename and by content - is covered by the file picker and live global search. Missing: a configured notes directory and the note-management commands (new note from the filter text, rename, delete). Zetteldeft's ID/link/tag map is entirely absent

the layer drives the external pandoc binary over the buffer; zmax pipes the buffer or selection through any external command (:pipe pandoc -f markdown -t rst). Missing: the pandoc-mode option hydra that builds the flags, and org export via ox-pandoc
embedded vimlrs (b_assert_beeps): runs {cmd} with errors captured and appends 'command did not beep' to v:errors when it does not error
embedded vimlrs (b_assert_fails): runs {cmd}, matches the captured error against {error}, and appends to v:errors when it does not fail
embedded vimlrs (b_assert_nobeep): the complement of assert_beeps()
`:` opens the command line (command_mode_count, which also takes the count prefix); an empty command line runs nothing, which is the whole of what the spec says `:` does

`:*` is the last Visual area as a range, resolved by the ex range parser (split_leading_range, resolve_filter_range), so every ranged ex command takes it: :*, :*sort, :*normal, :*!cmd, :*m/:*t. Unit test: star_range_is_the_last_visual_area

:lockvar [depth] {name} locks a variable: :let and :unlet on it fail with E741, checked in the command layer that owns both. vim's {depth} is accepted but the lock is on the name (vim's depth 1) - the value lives inside vimlrs and has no lock bit to set
:unlockvar [depth] {name} takes the :lockvar lock off; :let and :unlet accept the name again
c_CTRL-G: next match while 'incsearch' is live (ui/prompt.rs incsearch_cycle). vim presets only - in the Emacs presets C-g stays isearch-abort
c_CTRL-T: previous match while 'incsearch' is live (ui/prompt.rs incsearch_cycle)
dp = :diffput on the change under the cursor - the mirror of `do` (:diffget), which was already bound
i_CTRL-^ toggles 'iminsert', which is the switch the insert-mode self-insert reads before translating a typed character through the :lmap (Lang-Arg) table
c_CTRL-Q: like c_CTRL-V, the next key is data, not a command. vim presets only - in the Emacs presets C-q keeps exiting the completion selection

c_CTRL-\ e evaluates an expression through vimlrs and replaces the command line with the result. vim opens a nested `=` prompt to read {expr}; zmax evaluates the line already typed - the same text, one prompt fewer
c_CTRL-] expands the :cabbrev in front of the cursor (zmax_core::abbrev find_expansion, AbbrevMode::Command - the table :cabbrev fills)
c_CTRL-^ toggles 'imsearch', which is the switch ui/prompt.rs reads before translating a typed character through the :lmap (Lang-Arg) table
C-x 5 2: new tty frame (its own window tree and tab set) showing this buffer; only one frame is displayed at a time, as in a terminal Emacs
C-x 5 c: new frame holding a copy of this frame's whole layout - every tab, every split
C-x 5 0: deletes the displayed frame and displays another; refuses to delete the sole frame, as Emacs does
C-x 5 1: deletes every frame but the displayed one
C-x 5 o: displays the next frame (count honoured, wraps) - swaps the live window tree for that frame's
restores the most recently deleted frame; errors when undelete-frame-mode is off, because then nothing was recorded (as in Emacs)
gates whether delete-frame records the frame it deletes; turning it off also drops the recorded frames
picker over the frame names (set by set-frame-name) with each frame's focused buffer; selecting one displays it
renames the displayed frame - the name select-frame-by-name looks up
C-x 5 f: the file picker's open lands in a new frame, via the same pending-display mechanism Emacs's display-buffer uses
C-x 5 r: opens the file in a new frame and marks the document read-only
C-x 5 b: the picked buffer is displayed in a new frame
C-x 5 d: makes a new frame and opens Dired over it; partial because zmax Dired is a modal overlay, so it covers the frame rather than living in a window of it (the same caveat as dired-other-window)
C-x 5 5: arms Editor::pending_display, which Editor::switch consumes on the next buffer display and turns into a new frame
C-x 4 4: the next buffer display is redirected from the current window to a split
C-x t t: the next buffer display is redirected into a new tab
prompts for a buffer name, splits and shows it WITHOUT selecting the window (the difference from switch-to-buffer); with a count greater than one it pops the buffer into a new frame

shows the buffer in another window without selecting it, honouring the other-window/other-tab/other-frame prefix commands. Emacs's display-buffer-alist - the rule list that decides WHERE each buffer goes - is not implemented; zmax splits, or follows the armed prefix

binds $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/zmax/server (else $TMPDIR/zmax<uid>/server), removing a stale socket first, and hands the listener to the event loop, whose tokio::select! accepts on it; run again it stops the server and releases every waiting client. Divergence: the wire protocol is zmax's own line protocol (-auth/-dir/-file/-eval/-nowait), not emacsclient's
C-x #: saves the buffer synchronously, writes 'done' to every client blocked on it, closes their sockets and kills the buffer; with no client waiting it just saves and says so
writes 'abort' to the clients waiting on this buffer and disconnects them without saving
mints 64 characters from /dev/urandom, stores them on the running server and writes them to <socket>.key with mode 0600; from then on a client that does not send '-auth <key>' is refused
connects to another zmax server's socket as a client (reading its .key file if it has one), sends -eval, and reports the reply on the status line

moves point to the last click; the mouse-1 handler in ui::editor dispatches through this command, so it really is the mouse's handler (Editor::last_mouse_pos is the click argument Emacs passes as an event)
the region runs from the drag's anchor to the click; the mouse-1 drag handler dispatches through it, and a non-empty drag leaves the editor in Select mode so operators act on it
extends the region to the click and copies it to the mouse yank register; pressing again at the same position kills it. Bound to mouse-3 through the vim mousemodel=extend path
point moves to the click and the mouse yank register is pasted there; this is what middle-click runs
pastes the primary selection (zmax's mouse yank register, which a drag fills) at point, without moving point to the click
toggles Editor::mouse_wheel_mode, which the wheel branch of the mouse handler reads - with the mode off the wheel really does not scroll
opens the buffer menu (the buffer picker) from the mouse
finds the URL or e-mail covering the last click with zmax_core::goto_address and opens it in the external browser
anchors the secondary selection at the last click. Divergence: not bound to M-mouse-1, which is zmax's add-cursor click; the command takes the click position (or point)

sets the secondary selection from the anchor to the click and paints it with the secondary-selection face text property, so it is visible, survives point moving, and tracks edits through the ChangeSet
inserts the secondary selection's text at the click, leaving the secondary selection itself in place
copies the secondary selection to the kill ring; invoked again with it already saved, it deletes it
overwrite mode that overwrites the newline too, so the file's line layout is preserved
toggles Editor::context_menu_mode, read by the right-button branch of the mouse handler - with the mode off no popup menu appears

text hidden with the invisible text property (hide-ifdef-mode, selective-display, outline folds) is revealed while point is inside it and hidden again when point leaves; the check runs every frame in EditorView::render, the equivalent of Emacs's post-command-hook
when on, the event loop runs desktop-save on quit, writing every file-visiting buffer and its point to the desktop file

toggles the break-indent the document formatter reads, so a soft-wrapped continuation line lines up under the line it wrapped from. Partial: Emacs continues the whole line prefix (indentation AND a comment marker or list bullet); zmax carries the indentation only
every key the editor reads is appended to the named file (an empty name closes it); the hook is in EditorView::handle_event, so it really logs all keyboard input
M-o d: facemenu-set-default - the enriched-mode face map, live once M-x enriched-mode sets the major mode
M-o b: facemenu-set-bold
M-o i: facemenu-set-italic
M-o l: facemenu-set-bold-italic
M-o u: facemenu-set-underline
M-o o: facemenu-set-face (prompts for the face)
C-c TAB: sgml-tags-invisible - hide the markup, show only the text
C-c C-w: fortran-window-create-momentarily (mark column 72 until the next key)
C-c RET: goto-address-at-point - open the URL or e-mail at point in the browser
C-c C-j: term-line-mode
C-c C-k: term-char-mode
C-c C-q: term-pager-toggle
C-c C-c: log-edit-done - handled on its real chord by the commit-message buffer (ui/magit.rs MagitCommit::handle_event)
C-c C-a: log-edit-insert-changelog (same handler)
C-c C-d: log-edit-show-diff (same handler)
C-c C-f: log-edit-show-files (same handler)
C-c C-w: log-edit-generate-changelog-from-diff (same handler)
gud-tbreak. Emacs gives it two keys, C-c C-t and C-x C-a C-t; zmax's C-c C-t is doc-view-open-text, so only the C-x C-a alias is bound
gud-cont. The same split: zmax's C-c C-r is rerun_last_run, so only Emacs's C-x C-a C-r alias is bound
C-x =: what-cursor-position - reports the character at point, its codepoint and its coding
exchange-point-and-mark. In rectangle-mark-mode Emacs cycles the four corners; zmax swaps the selection's ends
SPC f v d: add-dir-local-variable - set a variable for the whole tree in .dir-locals.el
SPC F D: delete all other frames
SPC F O: open a dired buffer in another frame
g@: call 'operatorfunc'. vim applies it to the region the following motion covers; zmax applies it to the current selection (the engine already has that region), so the {motion} argument is not read
M--: negative prefix argument, -1 on its own; digits after it keep the sign (M-- 4 2 = -42), and it flips digits already typed (C-u 5 - = -5)

C-x RET t: sets the coding system the hosted terminal's output bytes are decoded with - ui/terminal.rs transcodes each PTY chunk to UTF-8 with a stateful incremental decoder (a read can split a multi-byte character) before vt100, which assumes UTF-8, parses it. Partial: zmax's OWN tty output is written as &str through the termina backend, which encodes UTF-8 unconditionally and exposes no byte hook, so this governs the terminal zmax hosts, not the outer tty it is displayed on

C-x RET k: sets the coding system typed characters are encoded into on the way to the hosted terminal's PTY (ui/terminal.rs key_to_bytes). Partial, the mirror of set-terminal-coding-system: the outer tty's key bytes are decoded by termina before zmax sees them, so there is nothing there to re-decode

C-x RET x: the coding system the bytes exchanged with the window system's clipboard are in. Read in zmax_view::clipboard::execute_command on both sides - the copy encodes with it, the paste decodes with it - so every command-based provider (pbcopy/pbpaste, wl-copy, xclip, xsel, win32yank) honours it at once

C-x RET X: the same, but consumed by the very next clipboard transfer only. coding::take_selection_coding() TAKES the one-shot, which is what makes it one-shot; the persistent selection coding applies again after

C-x RET p: reads a DECODE ENCODE pair (naming one uses it for both). Read by M-x shell (ui/comint.rs) for its stdout/stderr and its stdin, and by the :sh shell-command family (shell_impl). Fixed a real bug in the same place: comint read its pipes with read_line into a String, which errors on invalid UTF-8, and the error arm ended the reader thread - one stray byte silently killed all further output

C-x RET F: unix file names are bytes, not text, so a name held on disk in a non-UTF-8 coding can only be reached by encoding the typed name the same way. Editor::open now does that (encode_file_name -> OsStr::from_bytes). Windows names are UTF-16 at the API, so there is no byte layer and it is a no-op there

the coding system files are decoded with when opened: threaded into Document::open's encoding argument, which Editor::open had hardcoded to None. Partial: emacs pushes the coding to the front of a PRIORITY LIST its detector still consults; zmax has no multi-candidate detector to prioritise, so the preferred coding is used directly and clearing it restores BOM/charset detection

C-x RET c: arms a coding system for the next file read or write. Consumed by the next Editor::open (C-x RET c latin-1 RET C-x C-f reads that file as Latin-1) or the next Editor::save (C-x RET c koi8-r RET C-x C-s writes the buffer in KOI8-R) - the two uses the manual gives it

a file name decoded with the wrong coding system shows up mojibaked; this runs the same round trip recode-region does (encode back with the wrong coding to recover the bytes, decode with the right one) on the path, and really renames the file on disk, repointing the buffer at it

picks a language environment from a 26-entry table (Latin-1..9, Greek, Hebrew, Cyrillic-ISO/KOI8/ALT, Japanese, Chinese-GB/GB18030/BIG5, Korean, Thai, Turkish, Vietnamese) and sets the file, file-name, terminal, keyboard, selection and process coding systems to that language's coding at once. Partial: emacs's language environments also carry input methods, character sets and sample text; zmax takes the part that has an effect, the coding systems

resolves LC_ALL, then LC_CTYPE, then LANG (the order POSIX resolves them in), reads the codeset after the '.', and applies it as the language environment. Handles the POSIX codeset spellings the WHATWG label table does not know (glibc's eucJP, ISO8859-1)

M-x list-packages: fetches every archive's listing (GNU ELPA, NonGNU ELPA, MELPA) and opens the Package Menu over it. A zmax package is an emacs package: a directory of .el files under ~/.zmax/elpa/<name>-<version>/, put on the elisp load path and evaluated by the embedded elisprs interpreter. The archive-contents parser is a real elisp reader pinned by unit test to the bytes elpa.gnu.org actually serves

downloads <name>-<version>.tar (or .el) over HTTP, unpacks it into ~/.zmax/elpa/, and activates it by evaluating its <name>-autoloads.el (or <name>.el) in elisprs. Dependencies are resolved depth-first from the archive listing and installed before their dependants; 'emacs' is a host-version requirement, not a package, and is skipped, as is anything already installed at a new enough version
installs a package from a local file: a .tar (or .tar.gz - the gzip magic is sniffed) is unpacked as it stands, a bare .el becomes a package of that name versioned 0.0. Then activated like any other

puts every installed package on the elisp load path and evaluates its autoload file. Also runs at STARTUP (Application::load_init_scripts) before init.el, so an init file can configure a package it has installed - which is what package-initialize does in emacs. Only the newest installed version of each package is activated
installs the archive's newer version of one package; errors if the archive has nothing newer than what is on disk
installs a newer version of every package whose status is obsolete (the archive has something newer than what is installed)
writes ~/.zmax/package-quickstart.el, concatenating every installed package's autoload file, so startup does not have to walk the elpa directory - the same file emacs generates for the same reason

re-evaluates every .el file of an installed package and reports the first that fails. Partial, and the reason is stated in zmax_core::package: elisprs is an interpreter, there is no .elc and nothing to byte-compile. What byte-compiling actually CHECKS - that every form reads and evaluates - is what this does (the same precedent as dired-do-byte-compile)
the same for every installed package, reporting how many passed and which failed; same no-byte-compiler divergence
C-h P: shows a package's version, the version installed and where, its archive, kind, home page, maintainer, keywords, dependencies and summary, in a scratch buffer

C-h p: reads a keyword (completed from every keyword the archives' packages declare), filters the package listing to the packages carrying it and opens the menu on the result; refuses a keyword no package has rather than showing an empty list
w in the package menu: opens the :url the archive gives for the package under point in the system browser
RET in the package menu: describes the package under point
i: marks the package under point for installation and steps down, as emacs's marking commands do. package-menu-execute then installs it
d: marks the package under point for deletion and steps down
u (and DEL in the menu, which steps up instead of down): removes the mark from the package under point
U: marks every package the archive has a newer version of for installation - installing the newer version IS the upgrade
~: marks for deletion every installed version that a newer INSTALLED version supersedes (distinct from U, which is about versions in the archive)

x: carries out the marks. Deletions remove the package directory; installations resolve dependencies and go through the download job, then activate. Marks are cleared and the rows rebuilt against what is now on disk, so a row flips available -> installed without a second network round trip
?: shows the package menu's key bindings
H: hides the package under point from the listing
(: shows the packages hidden with H again (greyed), or hides them once more
/ n: narrows the listing to packages whose name contains the text. Filters COMPOSE - each one narrows what the previous left - as they do in emacs
/ d: narrows the listing to packages whose summary contains the text
/ N: narrows to packages whose name OR summary contains the text
/ k: narrows to packages declaring the keyword (the :keywords the archive lists)
/ s: narrows by the Status column - installed, available, obsolete (a newer version is in the archive) or external (installed but in no archive, i.e. from a file or from VC)
/ a: narrows to the packages one archive served (gnu, nongnu, melpa)
/ v: narrows to packages whose version starts with the text
/ m: shows only the packages carrying a mark - what x is about to act on
/ u: shows only the packages that can be upgraded
/ /: drops every filter, restoring the full listing

installs a package straight from its source repository instead of from an archive tarball: git clone into ~/.zmax/elpa/<name>-0.0/, then activate. Shells out to git, the same way zmax_loader::grammar fetches tree-sitter grammars. The result is listed, described, activated and deleted exactly like an archive package
clones a package's source into a directory you name WITHOUT installing it - for working on the package itself
makes an existing working copy an installed package by symlinking it into the elpa directory, so edits to the checkout are live - what emacs does, for the same reason
re-evaluates a VC package's elisp after its source changed and re-activates it. Partial for the same reason as package-recompile: 'built' means byte-compiled in emacs, and elisprs is an interpreter

runs git format-patch over your commits on a VC package, writes the patch series into the package's directory and opens the first. Partial: emacs MAILS the patches to the maintainer, and zmax has no SMTP transport (the same limit message-send documents), so the series is written to disk instead of sent

opens a message-mode draft addressed to the package's maintainer, with the package name, the installed version, its archive, its home page and the zmax version already filled in. Partial: sending queues to the local outbox - zmax has no SMTP transport
a left click moves the cursor to the clicked position (the Down(Left) branch of the mouse handler in ui/editor.rs dispatches through mouse_set_point)

a middle click moves the cursor to the click and pastes there (emacs mouse-2). Divergence from vim's gP: the text comes from the mouse-yank register, not the unnamed one, and the cursor lands after the pasted text
with mousemodel=extend the right button extends the selection to the click, which is vim's own rule for that value; with popup/popup_setpos (the default) it opens the context menu instead
the mouse handler is mode-independent: a left click in Insert moves the cursor to the click and stays in Insert

the wheel scrolls the window under the pointer in every mode, including Insert; the branch calls the same commands::scroll the scroll_down command runs, and honours vim 'mousescroll' (ver:N lines per notch)
as <ScrollWheelDown>, upwards; 'mousescroll' ver:N sets the notch size

the layer's whole job is remapping the command keys for a non-QWERTY layout (bepo, Colemak, Dvorak, Neo). `:set langmap=...` does exactly that - the translation runs before the keymap lookup, so every binding stays reachable

beancount journals get the full layer: tree-sitter grammar plus beancount-language-server. Ledger gets its grammar and indent but no server (none exists), so it is highlighting only. Missing for both: the ledger-mode command surface - reports, register, reconcile, balance - which shells out to the `ledger` binary
zmax reads mail (Rmail, with MIME) and composes it. Missing: maildir support and the `mu` index - mu4e's search query language and its threaded headers view are the layer, and both sit on that index
as mu4e: the reader and composer exist; the notmuch index (and therefore its saved searches, tags and thread view) does not
the mail half is covered by Rmail + compose-mail. Missing: the NNTP newsreader half, and Gnus's group/summary/article three-buffer model
message-goto-followup-to: go to the Mail-Followup-To header, creating it if the draft has none
message-goto-reply-to: go to the Reply-To header, creating it if the draft has none
message-goto-fcc: go to the Fcc header (the file the sent message is copied to), creating it if the draft has none
message-tab: completes the mail alias before point in a header line, an ordinary indent anywhere else (Insert only: in Normal, TAB is vim's jump_forward)
TAB updates the indentation of the current line: c-indent-line-or-region in a C/C++ buffer (Insert only, like Text mode's TAB)

DEL is backward-delete-char-untabify: a tab is first expanded to spaces, so DEL deletes one column of indentation. Bound in the C/C++ overlay (cc-mode reaches it through c-backspace-function); Insert only
major modes redefine TAB: org-cycle in Org, c-indent-line-or-region in C/C++, indent-for-tab-command in Text (keymap/major_mode.rs overlays the language's map on the base one)
major modes redefine DEL: backward-delete-char-untabify in a C/C++ buffer (the base map's delete_char_backward everywhere else)
major modes redefine C-j: tex-terminate-paragraph in TeX, eval-print-last-sexp in Emacs Lisp
mouse-set-point: mouse-1 on text moves point to the click. ui/editor.rs's Down(Left) arm runs this command, so the command really is the handler
mouse-yank-at-click: mouse-2 moves point to the click and inserts the kill ring's top there (ui/editor.rs's Down(Middle) arm runs it)
mouse-save-then-kill: mouse-3 extends the region to the click and copies it; a second press at the same place kills it (ui/editor.rs's Down(Right) arm, under mousemodel=extend)

universal-argument: C-u starts a prefix argument (bare C-u = 4, each further C-u multiplies by 4, digits replace it). Bound in the emacs preset, where C-u is emacs's key; in the shipped spacemacs/vim default C-u keeps vim's half-page-up

digit-argument: M-1..M-9 / M-0 read a numeric prefix argument. The digit is in the keystroke, so EditorView::handle_prefix_key folds it in directly (no keymap leaf can carry it); the command is what M-x digit-argument runs
negative-argument: M-- makes the prefix argument negative (-1 on its own, digits after it build a negative number). Consumed by EditorView::handle_prefix_key, like the digit keys
C-u C-SPC pops the mark ring: set_mark_command asks cx.prefix_arg() and runs pop_to_mark when an argument is present, instead of setting a mark
C-u C-x C-x exchanges point and mark WITHOUT reactivating the region: flip_selections asks cx.prefix_arg() and leaves Select mode when one is present
C-u M-; kills the comment on the line (comment-dwim with an argument): toggle_comments asks cx.prefix_arg() and runs comment_kill when one is present
SPC x A (link-hint-open-all-links): open_all_buffer_links scans the current buffer for URLs and opens each in the OS browser (capped at 50)
SPC x O (link-hint-open-link): link_hint_open_link pickers over the buffer's URLs; confirming one opens it in the browser
SPC x y (link-hint-copy-link): link_hint_copy_link pickers over the buffer's URLs; confirming one copies it to the clipboard register
SPC x Y (link-hint-copy-all-links): copy_all_buffer_links copies every URL in the buffer to the clipboard, one per line

SPC D d d (ediff-directories): ediff_directories prompts for two directories and renders the same-name files that differ, plus files unique to each side, into a scratch overview (top-level, like Emacs)
SPC D d 3 (ediff-directories3): ediff_directories3 prompts for three directories and lists the same-name files present in all three that are not byte-identical
SPC h P s: profiler_start resets and enables the command profiler, which instruments MappableCommand::execute (the single dispatch point) to time every command run
SPC h P k: profiler_stop disables the profiler, keeping its samples for the report
SPC h P r: profiler_report renders per-command call count / total ms / avg µs, slowest first, into a scratch buffer
SPC h P w: profiler_write_report prompts for a path and writes the profiler report text to it

SPC x r ': regexp_generate_strings prompts for a finite regexp and lists every string it matches; enumerate_regex parses a PCRE subset (literals, ( ), |, [ ], ?, {n,m}) and rejects infinite constructs
SPC x r p ': same finite-regexp string generator, PCRE syntax (regexp_generate_strings)
SPC x r e ': regexp_generate_strings_emacs translates Emacs regexp syntax (\( \| \{ \}) to the PCRE subset via emacs_regex_to_pcre_subset, then enumerates
SPC T T (toggle frame transparency): :toggle transparent-background flips the editor's transparent-background option, the terminal's only kind of transparency (the GUI window then shows through)
SPC T B (toggle frame background transparency): :toggle transparent-background, same terminal background-transparency toggle
SPC T f (fringe-mode): toggle_fringe hides/shows the whole gutter column strip (the terminal fringe), stashing and restoring the gutter layout
SPC t m T (toggle mode line itself): :toggle render-statusline turns the per-window status line off/on

SPC q r (restart-emacs): restart_editor refuses on unsaved buffers, else closes every view and sets a flag that main_impl reads after the UI loop exits (terminal restored) to re-exec the binary with the same arguments
duplicate inventory entry for SPC x r ' (generate strings from a finite regexp): regexp_generate_strings
duplicate inventory entry for SPC x r e ' (finite Emacs regexp): regexp_generate_strings_emacs
duplicate inventory entry for SPC x r p ' (finite PCRE regexp): regexp_generate_strings
profile functions/scripts -> command profiler (start/stop/dump)
stop profiling -> halt profiler keeping samples
scriptencoding hint; scripts read as UTF-8
M-s M-w word-search -> isearch_forward_word
fringe-mode -> toggle gutter/sign column (TUI fringe)
nroff-mode major mode
print-buffer -> ps_print_buffer (lpr)
print-region -> ps_print_region (lpr)
eww in-buffer web browser: fetch + HTML->text render
render local HTML file in eww buffer
web-search words and render results in eww buffer
open URLs in-editor via eww fetch+render
eww layer: in-editor web browser
translate word under cursor (google-translate-at-point)
set translate source/target languages
reverse translate languages
translate layer: machine translation via gtx endpoint
prev file in directory (reachable by name; the [f chord stays vim goto-file)
next file in directory (reachable by name; the ]f chord stays vim goto-file)
M-- M-u upcase previous word
M-- M-l downcase previous word
M-- M-c capitalize previous word
reposition-window: fit function on screen (z f chord stays vim create-fold)
bury current buffer
toggle 0/1-based column indexing in statusline
apply .editorconfig to buffer (auto on open; command re-applies)
leave view-mode
leave view-mode
rectangle editing via rectangle-mark-mode + C-x r family
C-x C-x in rectangle-mark-mode: opposite corner
run compile command (emacs compile)
universal_argument: C-u universal prefix argument (SPC u)
digraph entry via C-k (insert_digraph); different input model than {char1}<BS>{char2}
c_<LeftMouse>: a click on the : line puts the command-line cursor at the clicked column (Prompt::move_to_column — the inverse of the cursor's index->column mapping, anchor- and display-width-aware).
help: opens the inline Help browser (help-command entrypoint)
TAB indentation -> indent
CTRL-W z close preview window -> :pclose (C-w z chord itself is recenter_other_window)
ffap available as the find_file_at_point command (opens file/URL at point); not a find-file-rebinding minor mode
C-LeftMouse: go to definition of symbol at click
C-RightMouse: pop tag/jump stack (CTRL-T)
g<LeftMouse> = C-LeftMouse: go to definition at click
g<RightMouse> = C-RightMouse: pop tag/jump stack
real IRC client (connect/join/say/view transcript); manual-refresh buffer, not erc live multi-window UI
real IRC client via :irc-connect (rcirc-equivalent connectivity)
export Org buffer to Markdown (scratch or file)
C-c C-e in Org mode -> :org-export (also bound in the org major-mode overlay)
fold buffer by outline headings (Org * and Markdown #); toggle
<S-LeftMouse>: `*` at the click. search_selection_impl expands the bare cursor to the word under it.
<S-RightMouse>: `#` at the click.
[<MiddleMouse> = [p: a pending `[` prefix turns the middle click into paste-before at the click.
]<MiddleMouse> = ]p: a pending `]` prefix turns the middle click into paste-after at the click.
<S-ScrollWheelDown>: shift+wheel moves the window one page (inner_height) instead of mousescroll=ver:N lines.
<S-ScrollWheelUp>: shift+wheel moves the window one page up.
<ScrollWheelLeft>: horizontal wheel moves the window mousescroll=hor:N columns left (vim default hor:6).
<ScrollWheelRight>: horizontal wheel moves the window mousescroll=hor:N columns right.
<S-ScrollWheelLeft>: shift+horizontal wheel moves the window one page (inner_width) left.
<S-ScrollWheelRight>: shift+horizontal wheel moves the window one page right.

i_CTRL-_: toggle 'revins', gated on 'allowrevins' exactly as vim gates it. Bound to C-_ in the vim preset, where NON_VIM_INSERT frees the key; the shipped spacemacs default keeps C-_ = emacs undo, so this cites the command, not the key.
v_CTRL-G toggles Visual<->Select. zmax has one Select mode (Mode is Normal/Select/Insert), so there is no second mode to toggle to; select_mode is the single mode both map onto.
v_CTRL-O switches Select->Visual for one command. Same single-Select-mode model as v_CTRL-G; no distinct Visual mode exists to switch into.
C-M-y isearch-yank-char: extends the search with the next buffer char (prompt.rs isearch_yank IsearchYank::Char).
C-M-w isearch-yank-symbol-or-char: extends the search with the next word/symbol (prompt.rs isearch_yank IsearchYank::WordOrChar).
C-M-d isearch-del-char: drops the last char of the search string.
C-M-z isearch-yank-until-char: reads a char and extends the search up to it (next_char_handler).
M-s ' isearch-toggle-char-fold: `resume` matches `resume` with accents (IsearchToggle::CharFold).
M-s SPC isearch-toggle-lax-whitespace: a space matches any run of whitespace (IsearchToggle::LaxWhitespace).
M-s M-< isearch-beginning-of-buffer: restart the search from the buffer start, so it finds the first match in the whole buffer.
M-s M-> isearch-end-of-buffer: restart the search from the buffer end, so it finds the last match in the whole buffer.
i package-menu-mark-install: mark the package for installation (package_menu.rs:239).
d package-menu-mark-delete: mark the package for deletion (package_menu.rs:244).
u package-menu-mark-unmark: clear the package's mark (package_menu.rs:249).
U package-menu-mark-upgrades: mark every upgradable package (package_menu.rs:255).
~ package-menu-mark-obsolete-for-deletion: mark all obsolete packages (package_menu.rs:259).
x package-menu-execute: carry out the marked installs/deletions (package_menu.rs:268).
g revert-buffer: refresh the archive listing (package_menu.rs:277).
r package-menu-refresh: refresh the archive listing (package_menu.rs:277).
RET package-menu-describe-package: describe the package at point (package_menu.rs:286).
w package-browse-url: open the package's home page (package_menu.rs:294).
H package-menu-hide-package: hide the package (package_menu.rs:303).
( package-menu-toggle-hiding: show the hidden packages again (package_menu.rs:308).
? package-menu-quick-help: the quick help (package_menu.rs:321).
h package-menu-quick-help: the quick help (package_menu.rs:321).
/ n package-menu-filter-by-name (dispatch_filter_key, package_menu.rs:172).
/ d package-menu-filter-by-description (package_menu.rs:173).
/ N package-menu-filter-by-name-or-description (package_menu.rs:174).
/ k package-menu-filter-by-keyword (package_menu.rs:175).
/ s package-menu-filter-by-status (package_menu.rs:176).
/ a package-menu-filter-by-archive (package_menu.rs:177).
/ v package-menu-filter-by-version (package_menu.rs:178).
/ m package-menu-filter-marked: show only marked packages (package_menu.rs:181).
/ u package-menu-filter-upgradable: show only upgradable packages (package_menu.rs:186).
/ / package-menu-clear-filter: drop every filter (package_menu.rs:191).

C-x t is Emacs's tab-prefix-map -- a prefix, never a command, so it has no chord of its own to cite. zmax has the map populated (C-x t 0 close_tab, 1 tab_only, 2 new_tab, b switch_to_buffer_other_tab, f find_file_other_tab, m :tabmove, o goto_next_tabpage); cited via C-x t 2. keymap/spacemacs.rs keeps it a prefix on purpose -- giving it a command drops every chord underneath.
n doc-view-next-page.
p doc-view-previous-page.
next (PageDown) doc-view-next-page.
prior (PageUp) doc-view-previous-page.
PageDown doc-view-next-page.
PageUp doc-view-previous-page.
C-x ] doc-view-next-page.
C-x [ doc-view-previous-page.
SPC doc-view-scroll-up-or-next-page: the page is one terminal image, so there is nothing to scroll within and it advances a page.
DEL doc-view-scroll-down-or-previous-page; see SPC.
M-< doc-view-first-page.
M-> doc-view-last-page.
+ doc-view-enlarge (+25 dpi, clamped 25..600).
- doc-view-shrink (-25 dpi, clamped 25..600).
i + image-increase-size: scale up (5/4, the same step :image-increase-size uses).
i - image-decrease-size: scale down (4/5).
i r image-rotate: 90 degrees clockwise.
i h image-flip-horizontally.
i v image-flip-vertically.
s w image-transform-fit-to-window.
s o image-transform-reset-to-original: drop every rotate/flip/scale.
